question: An aircraft descends from FL165 to an altitude of 3 000 ft. According to the descent table, the distance covered is 37 NM in 13 minutes. If the aircraft experiences a tailwind component of 20 kt and flies in ISA conditions, then its average TAS is ___ and the actual descent distance covered is ___.

Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - VFR Chart ED-4
Find the magnetic course from WALDA 112.80 (WLD, N48º35’ E011º08’) to MUNICH 112.30 (MUN, N48º11’ E011º49’).
explanation:

Directly beneath the aerodrome designator EDDM, the following frequency information is provided:
Tower (North): 118.70 MHz
Tower (South): 120.50 MHz
ATIS: 123.12 MHz
When approaching from the south via the HOTEL 1, the pilot will most likely be handed over to Munich Tower (South) on 120.50 MHz.

explanation:

Below the designator LOWL (Linz) you can directly read the ATIS frequency: 128.12 MHz

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - VFR Chart ED-4
Find the frequency of LINZ ATIS to check the weather.
(Linz aerodrome: LOWL N48º15’ E014º11’)
Given a true track of 256° and a variation of 5°W, calculate the magnetic track:

Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - VFR Chart ED-4.
You are flying VFR, directly from airfield Deggendorf (EDMW) (N48°44' E012°53') to airfield Sonnen (EDPS) (N48°41' E013°42'). Chose the correct flight level for the flight:
explanation:
Refer to figure Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_47.

Based on the given coordinates, locate on the chart Freiburg airport (EDTF, N48°01.3’, E007°50.1’) and Offenburg airport (EDTO, N48°27.0’, E007°55.5’).
Draw a line which connects the two airports. This line represents the planned track.
In order to check for track deviation, we must select a unique landmark which is close to the planned track and can be located and identified from a typical altitude of a VFR flight. After determining, at the planning stage, the position of this landmark relative to the planned track and checking, during the flight, the aircraft position relative to selected landmark, the pilot will be able to determine if there is any drift from the planned track.
 
In order to check for time deviation, we must select either a line feature that is perpendicular to the planned track, e.g. a river or a road, or a unique and distinct landmark which is suitably on or as close as possible to the planned track and can be located and identified from a typical altitude of a VFR flight. By checking the actual time at which the aircraft passes overhead this landmark or crosses the line feature and comparing it with the estimated time overhead this landmark or the line feature, the pilot will be able to determine if there is a time deviation.
 
Keeping in mind the above and the planned track we have drawn, we can now examine each landmark separately:
 
The wind farm east of Altdorf-Wallburg (EDSW) airport:
According to the chart, this wind farm is located very close to the planned track. However, it is not advisable to select it for a drift and/or a time check. The reason is that the wind farm usually contains many wind power plants which are spread and as a result it is difficult to obtain an accurate position fix and an accurate time check.
 
The wind farm southwest of Schweighausen:
According to the chart, the wind farm southwest of Schweighausen is located to the east of the planned track. As in the case of the wind farm above, it should also not be used for a drift or a time check.
 
The river crossing the town of Herbolzheim:
According to the chart, the river that crosses the town of Herbolzheim is almost perpendicular the planned track and therefore it can be used to check for a time deviation. However, it cannot be used for a drift check because, during the flight, it is difficult to determine if you are crossing the river at the planned crossing point and therefore a second conspicuous landmark close to the crossing point is required. Please also note that the river is nearly indiscernible in the chart and so it may not be comfortably visible during the flight.
 
The unlighted tower north of the town of Emmendingen:
According to the chart, the unlighted tower north of Emmendingen is almost on the planned track. Its location together with the town is rather unique – a town with a railway and a tower/mast just north of it. Thus, the pilot, after passing Emmendingen, he/she should expect to pass slightly to the left of tower. The position of the aircraft relative to the tower will help him/her to determine any track deviation. Furthermore, since the unlighted tower is almost on the planned it can also be used for a time check. Therefore, among the given answers, the unlighted tower north of the town of Emmendingen is the best landmark for a drift and time check.
 
Æ Note 1: The term “wind farm” (or wind park) refers to a group of wind turbines in the same location which are used to produce electricity.
 
Æ Note 2: If you encounter this question in your official exams, please provide us any feedback that might help.

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - VFR Chart ED-4
A VFR flight is planned from Freiburg i. Br. airport (EDTF, N48°01.3’, E007°50.1’) to Offenburg airport (EDTO, N48°27.0’, E007°55.5’) at the lowest usable altitude. Which is a good landmark to check for drift and time deviation along your route?
options: A) The wind farm southwest of Schweighausen
B) The unlighted tower north of the town of Emmendingen
C) The wind farm east of Altdorf-Wallburg (EDSW) airport
D) The river crossing the town of Herbolzheim
correct_answer: B) The unlighted tower north of the town of Emmendingen

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - ALICANTE - Charts 19-1, 19-2, 19-3
You are approaching Alicante from the south, while flying near the coast. When do you have to contact ATC?
options: C) When crossing the ATZ boundary.
explanation:
QNH is 1 026.5 hPa, which is above the standard pressure 1 013 hPa:
Difference = 1 026.5 - 1 013 = 13.5 hPa

Pressure altitude correction = 13.5 × 30 ft/hPa = 405 ft
Since QNH is higher than standard, pressure altitude is lower than elevation:
Pressure altitude of airfield = 2 000 - 405 = 1 595 ft

Next, we need to calculate the height we need to climb:
FL120 = 12 000 ft pressure altitude
Height to climb = 12 000 - 1 595 = 10 405 ft

Calculate time to climb:
Time = Height / ROC = 10 405 / 1 000 = 10.405 minutes = 0.1734 hours

Finally, we need to calculate the ground speed:
Wind is 180°/20 kt, track is 360° (wind is directly from behind => tailwind).
Ground speed = TAS + tailwind = 222 + 20 = 242 kt

Ground distance = GS × Time = 242 × 0.1734 = 41.96 NM ≈ 42 NM

Note: this question has been created based on incomplete feedback. Please let us know if you encounter a similar question in your official exams.

question:
An aircraft departing from an airfield (elevation 2 000 ft) must reach FL120 with a rate of climb of 1 000 ft/min. Calculate the ground distance covered during the climb with the following data:
 
QNH:	1 026.5 hPa
Average TAS:	222 kt
Wind:	180° / 20 kt
True track during climb:	360°
1 hPa:	30 ft
explanation:
Refer to figure Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_91.
The correct answer is Munich ATIS, 123.12 MHz, because ATIS (Automatic Terminal Information Service) is specifically designed to provide pilots with key operational and meteorological information for an aerodrome in a continuous broadcast format.

ATIS includes essential details such as the name of the aerodrome, runway(s) in use, type of approach, visibility, cloud base, weather conditions, wind, temperature, dew point, altimeter setting, and other relevant information such as significant meteorological phenomena (e.g., wind shear), and trend forecasts when available.

Since we are looking for cloud base height, visibility, and the active runway at Munich, these are exactly the types of information provided on ATIS.

question: You are flying locally at EDNA with an unserviceable radio station and need cloud base height, visibility, and the active runway at Munich. What frequency should you use for this information?
explanation:
Refer to figure Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_34.
The question asks for the minimum altitude for this VFR that satisfies the requirements for obstacle clearance and can be maintained during the whole flight.
Based on the given coordinates, locate on the chart the departure airport REGENSBURG-OBERHUB (EDNR, N49° 08.52' E012° 04.92') and the destination airport STRUNKOVICE (LKSR, N49°4.95' E14°4.55').
With the help of a plotter, draw a line that connects the two airports.
From the chart, we can see that the route passes from an area where there is a spot elevation of 4 777 ft. Since the minimum altitude of a VFR flight shall be 500 ft above the ground when it is not flying over the congested areas of cities, towns or settlements or over an open-air assembly of persons, adding 500 ft to 4 777 feet, we get 5 277 feet. Rounding up this altitude to the next 100 feet increment, we get 5 300 feet. So, the minimum altitude for this route is 5 300 ft.
 
Æ Note 1: In a VFR flight, you are not required to fly according to the minimum grid area altitudes because a minimum grid area altitude might be unnecessarily high. The altitude you will choose to fly shall satisfy the following regulations: 

Easy Access Rules for Standardized European Rules of the Air (SERA)
 
SERA.5005 Visual flight rules
 
(f) Except when necessary for take-off or landing, or except by permission from the competent authority, a VFR flight shall not be flown:
over the congested areas of cities, towns or settlements or over an open-air assembly of persons at a height less than 300 m (1 000 ft) above the highest obstacle within a radius of 600 m from the aircraft;
elsewhere than as specified in (1), at a height less than 150 m (500 ft) above the ground or water, or 150 m (500 ft) above the highest obstacle within a radius of 150 m (500 ft) from the aircraft.
Æ Note 2: Please let us know if this question appears in your exams, what possible answers were given and which answer is marked as correct.

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - VFR Chart ED-4
A VFR flight is planned from Germany to Czech Republic. The departure airport is REGENSBURG-OBERHUB (EDNR, N49°08.5' E012°04.9') and the destination airport is STRUNKOVICE (LKSR, N49°05.0' E14°04.5'). What is the minimum altitude which should be maintained during the flight?

Along the route there is an obstacle with an elevation of 13 421 ft above MSL. If the QNH is 989 hPa, the magnetic track is 311º and the temperature is 10º below ISA, the lowest appropriate VFR flight level that you could choose in order to clear this obstacle by a minimum of 2 000 ft is:

Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - NANTES - Charts 19-1.
An aircraft is departing from Nantes for a VFR flight to the Northwest and will cross waypoint 'NW'. What will be the best way to check the position at waypoint 'NW' visually?
options: A) By checking your position using GNSS.
B) The lighted mast next to waypoint NW.
C) By checking your position with bearing and distance from NTS VOR/DME.
D) By looking for the highway junction which is the only junction in that area.
correct_answer: D) By looking for the highway junction which is the only junction in that area.

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - VFR Chart ED-4
You are flying VFR in the vicinity of airfield Albstadt Degerfeld (EDSA, 48°15’N, 009°04'E). Which of the following options offers the most suitable visual landmark?
options: A) One of the T-junctions of the secondary roads about 9 NM east of Alsbstadt.
B) The natural high point with elevation 2 638 ft about 8 NM east of Albstadt.
C) The village TROCHTELFINGEN about 8 NM north-east of Albstadt.
D) The T-junction of the railway at the village GAMMERTINGEN, about 6 NM east of Albstadt.
correct_answer: D) The T-junction of the railway at the village GAMMERTINGEN, about 6 NM east of Albstadt.

question:
An aircraft is flying VFR using a VOR to navigate. The aircraft is following Radial 128º outbound from the VOR. What is the expected Wind Correction Angle (WCA)?
 
Wind:	080º(T)/35 kt
TAS:	155 kt
Variation:	15ºW
What is the highest elevation within 5 NM on either side of the route shown?
explanation:
Refer to figure 2013.
Using the given information, we can calculate the wind correction angle (WCA) and the Ground speed (GS) with the help of the triangle of velocities or using a flight computer:
 
Calculation of wind correction angle and of Ground speed with the help of the triangle of velocities (trigonometry):
Using a ruler and a protractor, draw the triangle of velocities. Remember that the tip of the True airspeed/True heading vector must be on the tail on the W/V vector.
Consider: AB: Ground speed/True course vector, AC: True airspeed/True heading vector, BC: W/V vector.
Starting from point B, extend the Ground speed/True course vector (AB).
Starting from point C, draw a line at right angles to extended Ground speed/True course vector (AB).
Consider D the point of intersection of this line with the extended Ground speed/True course vector (AB). 
Since True course = 271° and True wind direction = 340°, we have: angle CBD = 340° - 271° = 69°

In the triangle BCD we have:
 
sin (CBD) = XWC (CD)/Wind speed ⇒ XWC = Wind speed x sin (CBD) = 20 x sin (69°) kt = 18.67 kt
 
cos (CBD) = HWC (BD)/Wind speed ⇒ HWC = Wind speed x cos (CBD) = 20 x cos (69°) kt = 7.17 kt
 
Consider “a” the angle between the Ground speed/True course vector (AB) and the True airspeed/True heading (AC) vector.
 
In the triangle ACD we have: 
 
sin (a) = XWC (CD)/ True airspeed = 18.67/135 ⇒ a = 7.95°
 
Since the True airspeed/True heading vector (AC) is to the right of Ground speed/True course vector (AB), we have:
 
Wind correction angle (WCA) = 7.95° right or +7.95°
 
Furthermore, in the triangle ACD we have:
 
cos (a) = AD/True airspeed ⇒ AD = True airspeed x cos (a) = 135 x cos (7.95°) kt = 133.95 kt (Effective TAS)

Thus, for the Ground speed (AB), we have:

Ground Speed = Effective TAS - HWC = 133.95 kt - 7.17 kt = 126.78 kt
 
Æ Note: HWC: Headwind component, XWC: Crosswind component.
 
Calculation of wind correction angle and of Ground speed using a flight computer:
 
E6-B flight computer:
Move the slide until the ring (grommet) falls over the 100 kt speed arc. Please note that this step is only added for an easier drawing of the W/V line (step 3).
Rotate the transparent (azimuth) disc to bring the true wind direction (340°) below the TRUE INDEX.
Starting from the ring, draw an upward line, along the centerline, with a length equal to the wind speed (20 kt). The line drawn represents the W/V vector.
Rotate the transparent (azimuth) disc to bring the true course (271°) below the TRUE INDEX. Thus, the Ground speed/True course vector is set on the centerline.
Move the slide until the end of the line falls over the true airspeed (TAS) speed arc of 135 kt. Thus, the triangle of velocities is completed.
Determine the wind correction angle (WCA). We can see that the end of the line drawn is approximately 8° to the right of the centerline. This means that the True airspeed/True heading vector is 8° to the right of the Ground speed/True course vector. As a result, we have: Wind correction angle = 8° right or +8°
Determine the Ground speed (GS) from the speed arc on which the ring (grommet) falls. In this question we can see that the ring falls on the 127 kt speed arc. As a result, we have: Ground speed = 127 kt
Æ Note 1: If you use an E6-B flight computer, you must have the following in mind:
If the question gives track or course, the W/V line must be drawn upwards.
If the question gives heading, the W/V line must be drawn downwards.
CR-5 flight computer:
Since TAS = 135 kt and wind speed = 20 kt, we have:
 
The lowest possible Ground speed is: Ground speed = TAS - wind speed = 135 kt - 20 kt = 115 kt
The highest possible Ground speed is: Ground speed = TAS + wind speed = 135 kt + 20 kt =155 kt
 
Since the lowest possible Ground speed and the highest possible Ground speed are both lower than 300 kt, we will use to low speed (L) side of the slide.
Move the slide to position the center dot on the 100 kt speed arc.
Rotate the window disc to align the true wind direction (300°) with the TRUE HEADING index on the fixed scale.
Starting from the center dot, draw a downward line, along the centerline, with a length equal to the wind speed (20 kt).
Rotate the window disc to align the true course (271°) with the TRUE HEADING index on the fixed scale.
Move the slide to position the center dot on the true airspeed (TAS) arc of 135 kt.
We can see that the end of the line drawn is approximately 8° to the left of the centerline. Therefore, rotate the window disc until the true course (271°) on the window disc is aligned with the line on the fixed scale that corresponds to 8° left drift angle.
We can see now that the end of the line drawn is still approximately 8° to the left of the centerline. This means that the drift angle on the fixed scale that is opposite of the true course (271°) on the window disc is the same as the degrees the end of the line is to the left of the centerline. Thus, the triangle of velocities is created and the flight computer is ready for reading the WCA and the GS.
Determine the true heading by the value on the window disc which is opposite to the TRUE HEADING index on the fixed scale. In this question, we can see that the value 279° on the window is opposite to the TRUE HEADING index. As a result, we have: True heading = 279°
Determine the wind correction angle (WCA). We have True course = 271° and true heading = 279°, i.e. the true heading is 8° to the right of the true course. As a result, we have: Wind correction angle (WCA) = 8° right or +8°
Determine the Ground speed (GS) from the speed arc passing below the end of the line drawn. In this question we can see that the speed arc which passes below the end of the line drawn corresponds to 127 kt. As a result, we have: Ground speed = 127 kt
Æ Note 1: The triangle of velocities is completed and the flight computer is ready for reading the WCA and the Ground speed when the drift angle on the fixed scale, which is opposite to the desired track/course on the window disc, is the same as the degrees the end of the line drawn is to the left or to the right of the centerline.
 
Æ Note 2: The procedure described above is according to the Pooleys CRP-5 flight computer manual. If you use a CRP-5 flight computer, you can also follow the procedure described for E6-B flight computer.
 
CR-3 flight computer
Set the TAS index below 13.5 (13.5 represents the true airspeed of 135 kt).
Rotate the green scale until the true course (271°) is above the TC index.
Find the 340° (wind direction) line on the green scale and on the point where this line intersects the 20 kt (wind speed) circle make a pencil mark.
From the pencil mark we can see that there is a right crosswind component of 19 kt which, according to the flight computer’s outer scale, corresponds to a crab angle of 8°.
Since there is a right crosswind component, the true heading is 8° to the right of the true course and we have: Wind correction angle = 8° left or +8°
Determine the Ground speed (GS). From the pencil mark we can see that there is a headwind component of approximately 7 kt. Thus, using the effective true airspeed, we have for the Ground speed:
Ground speed = Effective TAS - Headwind component = TAS x cos (WCA) - Headwind component = 135 x cos (8°) kt - 7 kt = 126.94 kt
Æ Note 1: In the 6th step of the solution (CR-3 flight computer), we used the Effective true airspeed for the calculation of the Ground speed. Since the wind correction angle (WCA) is lower than 10°, we can also use the true airspeed for the calculation of the Ground speed (Ground speed = 135 kt - 7 kt = 128 kt)
 
Æ Note 2: If this question appears in your official exams, please provide us with any feedback that might help.

question:
Given:
Cruise altitude:	6 500 ft
True course:	271°
True airspeed:	135 kt
Wind:	340°(T) / 20 kt

The wind correction angle (WCA) and ground speed (GS) is...
options: A) 8° right, and GS is 127 kt
B) 8° left, and GS is 127 kt
C) 8° left, and GS is 144 kt
D) 8° right, and GS is 144 kt
correct_answer: A) 8° right, and GS is 127 kt
explanation:
Refer to figure VFR_Alicante_19-1_Expl_9.
The maximum altitude for VFR arrivals and departures is given in a box depicted about mid-height on the right edge of the chart and reads “ARR/DEP MAX 1 500’”. This means a maximum altitude of 1 500 ft AMSL for VFR approaches along every route which has no other limit specified (which is only true for the abnormal procedure for comm failure which is flown at 800 ft). The box reading MAX 2 000’ depicted by Tabarca route is a maximum altitude for VFR flights in this sector, but not applicable to approaches.

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - ALICANTE - Charts 19-1, 19-2, 19-3
You are approaching from the southeast via reporting point SE and cleared via Tabarca route, then via South route. What is your maximum allowed altitude?
explanation:
Refer to figure Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_06.
Draw a line between both airfields and measure the true course along a Meridian which is approximately in the middle of both locations. Exactly 270°. See also attached explanation figure.

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - VFR Chart ED-4
Find the true track for a flight from FREISTADT in Austria (LOLF, N48° 30.9' E14° 24.5') to FÜRSTENZELL in Germany (EDMF, N48° 31.1' E013° 20.8' ):

Given:
 
TAS:	150 kt
Reported wind:	204° / 30 kt
Course:	200° (M)
Variation:	10° (W)

Calculate the ground speed.
explanation:
The first step is to find both points and draw a line between them.

Then, with the help of a plotter, measure the true track from the chart: align the plotter with a meridian line and read it directly: 273º

Now, since the question asks for magnetic track, we need to look for the variation and apply it to the true track. Midway through the straight line, we can find the isogonal stating there's a 2.5ºE variation. Using the mnemonic "variation east, magnetic least" we need to subtract variation from true track to get magnetic track:

273º - 2.5º = 270.5º ≈ 270º

For the distance calculation, remember that the distance between two parallels of latitude along a meridian line is 60 NM. Using that as a scale, simply measure the distance along the line connecting the two points: 49 NM.

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - VFR Chart ED-4.
Determine magnetic track and distance Schönberg (EDPK, 48°02'52""N 012°30'02""E) to Oberpfaffenhofen (EDMO, 48°04'53""N 011°16'59""E)?
explanation:
Refer to figures Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_68 and Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_98.
When flying VFR toward Munich from the northwest and instructed by ATC to hold before entering Class C airspace, the most appropriate and useful radio aid for maintaining a holding pattern would be the WLD VOR/DME. This is because WLD (Walda) is located directly northwest of Munich, within proximity to the Class C airspace boundary. It is clearly marked on the VFR navigation chart and includes both VOR and DME capabilities.

"GND" is the indication of "ground" in the restricted area ED-R 143 east of WLD VOR/DME when referring to its vertical extent (ground until 2 500 ft).

question: Flying VFR towards Munich and preparing to enter Class C airspace from the northwest, which radio aid would be most useful for maintaining a holding pattern in the vicinity of Class C airspace after being instructed by ATC to hold, until clearance is received?
explanation:
Refer to figure VFR_Aberdeen_19-1_Expl_1.
Refer to the upper right side of the chart and locate the section with the ATIS frequency information. 
The relevant section contains two frequencies: 114.300 MHz and 121.850 MHz
The frequency 114.300 MHz can be used when the aircraft is on the ground and in flight.
The frequency 121.850 MHz can be used only when the aircraft is on the ground (for ground use only).
 
From the above, we can conclude that the following frequencies are available when on the ground:
114.300 MHz and 121.850 MHz
 
The frequency 121.700 MHz is not an ATIS frequency. It is the frequency that must be used when the aircraft is on the ground.

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - ABERDEEN (Dyce) - chart 19-1
What ATIS frequency(ies) is/are available when on the ground?

Given:
 
Cruising level:	FL410
Temperature (OAT):	ISA +10 °C
Mach number:	M 0.86
Wind component:	-25 kt

Calculate the ground speed.
explanation:
Refer to figure Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_07.
Note that the location of each navigation aid is shown on the back of the ED(4) chart, so it is easier to locate and you do not need to plot each position.
Based on the given coordinates, locate FREISTADT aerodrome (LOLF, N48°31’, E014°25’) and RIED KIRCHHEIM aerodrome (LOLK, N48°13’, E013°21’).
Draw a line that connects the two aerodromes. This line represents the direct route between the two aerodromes.
Locate the position of each possible answer on the chart:
The position N48°14', E014°06' corresponds to LINZ (LNZ) VOR/DME.
The position N48°14', E014°19' corresponds to LINZ (LNZ) NDB.
The position N48°24', E013°27’ corresponds to SCHARDING SUBEN (LOLS) aerodrome.
The position N48°26’, E014°08' corresponds to FREISTADT (FRE) VOR/DME.
 
Since the pilot decided to navigate mainly with radio navigation aids, the SCHARDING SUBEN (LOLS) aerodrome is excluded from being a possible correct answer. Among the remaining possible answers, we can see that FREISTADT (FRE) VOR/DME (N48°26’, E014°08') is closest to the direct route between the two aerodromes and as a result it will give the most direct routing based on radio navigation aids from LOLF to LOLK.

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - VFR Chart ED-4
You are planning a VFR flight in Austria from Freistadt aerodrome (LOLF, N48°31’, E014°25’) to Ried Kirchheim aerodrome (LOLK, N48°13’, E013°21’). Your aircraft is equipped with radio navigation instruments. Since you are unfamiliar with this area, you decide to navigate mainly with radio navigation aids. The most direct routing based on radio navigation is from LOLF to ___ to LOLK.

Refer to a VFR flight from Schärding Süben (LOLS, 48°24'11"N, 013°26'53"E) to Pfarrkirchen (EDNP, 48°25'12"N, 012°51'53"E) and select the correct statements:
Recommended cruising altitude: FL055.
The aircraft will enter class F airspace.
The aircraft will enter class C airspace.
Recommended cruising altitude: FL065.
The crew can follow the Rott river to reach destination.
The crew can follow the A3 motorway to reach destination.

Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - Aberdeen 10-1V and 10-1VA.
You are flying with the railway line on the left, what altitude restriction apply?
options: A) At or below 2 000 ft on Aberdeen QNH.
B) At 2 000 ft QNH to avoid instrument traffic.
C) Not below 2 000 ft QNH except in an emergency.
D) At or below 2 000 ft above ground level (AGL).
correct_answer: A) At or below 2 000 ft on Aberdeen QNH.
explanation:
Refer to figures VFR_Alicante_19-1_Expl_2 and VFR_Alicante_19-3_Expl_1.
The procedure for aircraft with radio communications failure is specified in the chart. The prescribed track can be seen about centrally at the bottom of the chart. The dotted line labelled “ACFT with radio failure” instructs pilots to proceed in the direction of SW reporting point, then follow a track east of the South Route. The specified maximum altitude is 800 ft AMSL.

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - ALICANTE - Charts 19-1, 19-2, 19-3
When approaching ALICANTE, what is the procedure if you experience a communication failure?
options: A) Turn to the closest aerodrome without control zone, land there and inform ATC via phone call
B) Set transponder 7600, proceed according to your filed flight plan if no instructions have been received previously, otherwise follow latest ATC instructions
C) Maintain heading and altitude for 7 minutes, then descend to 2 000 ft and proceed to closest VFR reporting point and hold, waiting for light signals
D) Proceed in the direction of SW reporting point, assume a track east of South Route to S reporting point and maintain a maximum of 800 ft AMSL
correct_answer: D) Proceed in the direction of SW reporting point, assume a track east of South Route to S reporting point and maintain a maximum of 800 ft AMSL

question:
You are on a VFR flight and the traffic circuit altitude of your destination has to be established 5 NM before reaching the aerodrome. 

Given:
Ground Speed:	130 kt
Cruising altitude:	4 500 ft AMSL
Traffic circuit altitude:	2 500 ft AMSL
Rate of descent:	500 ft/min

Calculate the shortest distance from the destination where you must initiate the descent.

Given:
 
Track:	013º(T)

question:

An aircraft is flying between the reporting points N and NE inbound Alicante airport. What is the approximate distance between the reporting points N and NE and the magnetic track of the aircraft?
explanation:
Initial wind: FROM 340° at 15 kt, with a track of 330°.

The angle between the wind FROM direction and the track is only 10°, meaning the wind is almost directly on the nose. This produces a strong headwind component (15 × cos 10° ≈ 14.8 kt) and a very small crosswind component from the right (15 × sin 10° ≈ 2.6 kt). This is consistent with the given GS of 80 kt being lower than the TAS of 90 kt.

New wind: FROM 190° at 10 kt. The wind is now blowing toward 010°.

The angle between the wind FROM direction (190°) and the track (330°) is 140°. Since this is greater than 90°, the wind now has a tailwind component: 10 × cos(180° − 140°) = 10 × cos 40° ≈ 7.7kt.

The ground speed will therefore increase => from 80 kt with the original headwind to approximately 98 kt with the new tailwind.

For the drift:

The new wind blows toward 010°, which is to the right of the 330° track. This pushes the aircraft to the right of its intended track. To compensate, the pilot must apply a wind correction angle to the left, heading into the wind to maintain the desired track of 330°.

explanation:
Refer to figure Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_100.
To determine your distance from EDST Hahnweide during the climb, you must rely on basic flight calculations rather than navigation aids or ATC services. The reason is that EDST Hahnweide does not have a DME, so you cannot measure distance directly using DME equipment. Additionally, the only published frequency for EDST is 123.25 MHz, which is an Information frequency, not a Tower frequency, so radar vectors from ATC are not available when departing this airfield.

Calculating distance based solely on Indicated Airspeed (IAS) is not accurate, because IAS does not account for wind. For example, in the presence of a headwind, your ground speed (GS) will be significantly lower than your IAS, which means you will cover less horizontal distance during your climb than IAS would suggest.

The most reliable method in this case is to estimate the distance by using climb rate, time, and ground speed. By knowing how long you’ve been climbing and your actual ground speed, you can calculate how far you’ve traveled horizontally from the airport. Here’s a numerical example to illustrate:

Let’s assume:
Rate of Climb (ROC) = 500 feet per minute
Target altitude (e.g., top of climb) = 3 167 ft, which is 2 000 feet above aerodrome elevation (1 167 ft)
Ground speed = 60 knots
Step 1: Calculate time to climb (in minutes):
Time = Altitude difference ÷ ROC = 2 000 ÷ 500 = 4 minutes

Step 2: Convert time to hours for the next calculation:
4 ÷ 60 = 0.0667 hours

Step 3: Calculate distance = GS × Time
Distance = 60 × 0.0667 = 4.002 NM
So, after climbing to 2 000 ft AGL at 500 fpm with a GS of 60 knots, you will be approximately 4 NM from EDST.

This method is simple, wind-corrected (since it uses GS), and effective when no navigation aid is available. Therefore, estimating distance based on climb rate, time, and ground speed is the correct and most appropriate method in this scenario.

question: You are climbing straight ahead from EDST (N48°37.9′ E009°25.8′). What method would you use to find the distance of the aircraft from the airfield during ascent?
options: A) Estimating distance based on climb rate, time and ground speed.
B) Using radar vectors from ATC.
C) Using DME from the departure airport.
correct_answer: A) Estimating distance based on climb rate, time and ground speed.
explanation:
Refer to figure 1809.
In order to calculate the drift angle, the course and the wind direction must be in reference to the same North, True or Magnetic. Since the question refers to a flight, we can safely assume that the given wind is a route wind which is commonly given as True as the source of these winds is wind charts. So, in order to calculate the drift angle we have to convert either the course from Magnetic to True or the wind direction from True to Magnetic. In this explanation, we will convert the course from Magnetic to True.
 
From the question we have:
 
Variation = 20°W ⇒ Magnetic North is 20° West of True North ⇒ we have: True course = Magnetic course - 020° = 210° - 020° = 190°
 
Thus, given that TAS = 179 kt, True course = 190° and W/V: 330°/50 kt, with the help of the triangle of velocities or using a flight computer we get:
Drift angle = 10° left
Æ Note: If we consider the wind direction as magnetic, there is no need to use the given variation. In this case we get a 14º left drift angle which is not among the given answers.
 
Calculation of drift angle with the help of the triangle of velocities (calculation method):
Using a ruler and a protractor, draw the triangle of velocities. Remember that the tip of the True airspeed/True Heading vector must be on the tail on the W/V vector.
Consider: AB: Ground speed/True course vector, AC: True airspeed/True heading vector, BC: W/V vector.
Starting from point C, draw a straight line which is at right angles to the Ground speed/True course vector (AB).
Consider D the point of intersection of this line with the Ground speed/True course vector. 
Since True course = 190° and Wind direction = 330°, we have: angle CBD = 40°

In the triangle BCD we have:
sin (CBD) = Crosswind Component (CD) /Wind velocity ⇒ Crosswind component (CD) = Wind velocity x sin (CBD) = 50 x sin (40°) kt = 32.14 kt
Consider a the angle between the Ground speed/True course vector and the True airspeed/True heading vector.
 
In the triangle ACD we have: 
sin (a) = Crosswind Component (CD)/ True airspeed = 32.14/179 ⇒ a = 10.34° 
Since the Ground speed/True course vector is to the left of the True airspeed/True heading vector, we have:
Drift angle = 10.34° left

Calculation of drift angle using a flight computer:
 
Move the slide to position the center dot on the True airspeed arc of 179 kt.
Rotate the window disc to align the Wind direction (330°) with the TRUE HEADING index on the fixed scale.
Starting from the center dot, draw a downward line, along the centerline, with a length equal to the Wind velocity (50 kt).
Rotate the window disc to align the True course (190°) with the TRUE HEADING index on the fixed scale.
We can see that the end of the line is 8° the left of centerline. Therefore, rotate the window disc to show 198º under the true heading index.
We can see now that the end of the line is 10° to the left of the centerline.
Drift angle = 10° left.

The planned route is from Albstadt Degerfeld aerodrome to Laupheim aerodrome. From the chart, we have: 
At Albstadt Degerfeld (EDSA) airport, the aircraft can contact Degerfeld info on 125.10 MHz for flight information service.
After take-off, the aircraft is flying within a sector of LANGEN EDGG FIR and in this sector the aircraft can contact Langen Information on 128.95 MHz for flight information service. This can be seen from the information sign located southeast of Albstadt Degerfeld (EDSA) aerodrome and north of the grey FIR boundary line.
As the aircraft exits this sector of LANGEN EDGG FIR, it enters MUNCHEN EDDM FIR where, according to the chart, flight information service is still provided by Langen Information on 128.95 MHz.
 After a short while, the aircraft leaves this sector, and enters another sector of MUNCHEN EDMM FIR, where, according to the chart, flight information service is provided by Munchen information on 126.95 MHz. The aircraft can remain on this frequency until contact is established with Laupheim (Lima) Tower on 122.10 MHz.
Thus, based on the above and taking into consideration the possible answers, the correct answer is:
 
Langen Information 128.95 MHz and Munchen Information 126.95 MHz

Refer to the attached semicircular rules for VFR:

- When flying VFR we fly intermediate flight levels, e.g. FL55, FL65, FL75, etc, excluding options "FL50" and "FL 60" (this would correspond to IFR levels).
- We're flyinh an easterly track, meaning. we need to comply with an odd flight level, excluding option "FL65".

So, the only right option is FL55.

question: For a VFR flight planned from aerodrome A to aerodrome B along an 085° track, what flight level would be appropriate for this route?
explanation:
Refer to figure 2832.


Using the table, we can make a direct reading to find the NAM and the time required for a descent from 5 000 ft to the ground: 18 NAM and 6 min.
For the descent from 35 000 ft to ground, the time required for descent is 20 min, and after interpolating for weight:

Weight difference = 45 000 - 35 000 = 10 000 kg
NAM difference = 96 - 84 = 12 NAM
So, for 42 500 kg: 
NAM = 7 500 x 12 / 10 000 = 9 NAM (+ 84 = 93 NAM)

Descent from 35 000 ft to 5 000 ft:
NAM: 93 - 18 = 75 NAM
Time: 20 - 6 = 14 min

TAS = Distance / Time
TAS = 75 / (14/60) = 321 kt
GS = 321 - 21 = 300 kt

question: VFR flights shall not be flown over the congested areas of cities at a height less than:
options: A) 1 000 ft above the highest obstacle within a radius of 600 m from the aircraft.
B) 500 ft above the highest obstacle.
D) 2 000 ft above the highest obstacle within a radius of 600 ft from the aircraft.
correct_answer: A) 1 000 ft above the highest obstacle within a radius of 600 m from the aircraft.
explanation:
Refer to figures 373 and Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_90.
To determine the appropriate cruising level, we must first identify the minimum safe altitude along the route. According to the VFR chart and provided information, the Minimum Grid Area Altitudes (MGAAs) intersected by the flight path are 5 400 ft, 5 900 ft, 7 200 ft, 4 100 ft, and 4 700 ft. Among these, the highest is 7 200 ft, which represents the minimum altitude that ensures safe terrain and obstacle clearance throughout the route.

In VFR flight planning, however, terrain clearance is not the only consideration. The ICAO semicircular rule for VFR cruising altitudes must also be followed. According to this rule, for a flight with a magnetic track between 000° and 179° (easterly direction), the cruising level must be odd thousands of feet + 500 ft, for example: 3 500 ft, 5 500 ft, 7 500 ft, 9 500 ft, etc. The magnetic track of the route from Straubing to Linz is approximately 120°, which falls clearly into the easterly category. Therefore, the chosen cruising level must comply with the "odd + 500" rule.

Now, combining both factors:
The altitude must be at or above 7 200 ft to satisfy the obstacle clearance requirement.
The altitude must be a VFR level compliant with the semicircular rule for an easterly heading.
Among the options given, the first VFR-compliant level above 7 200 ft is 9 500 ft, which corresponds to FL095 in standard pressure settings.

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - ALICANTE - Charts 19-1, 19-2, 19-3

explanation:
Refer to figure Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_10.
Draw a line between RDG VOR/DME and airfield Thalmassing Waizenhofen.

To measure the True Course:
Read the magnetic course at the compass rose of RDG (270°) and add the variation of 2.5°E (dashed blue line) = 272.5º; or
​​Measure the true course at the Meridian between both locations (272°)
To measure distance:
Departure = change of long (min) x COS mean Lat
-In this case, the change of longitude is 12º31.5' - 11º12.57' = 1º19' = 1.32º
-Multiply by 60 to convert it into minutes. 1.32º x 60 = 79.2 min

Departure = 79.2 x cos (49º) = 52 NM
 
​Alternatively, find the distance by comparing the length of your line along a Meridian. Latitude information shows that one grit measures a distance of 30' (1 dash = 1 NM). The total distance is approximately 52 NM.

Æ note: It is possible that the chart or chart excerpt in your official examination will not be from the Jeppesen GSPRM but you can solve the question in a similar way - measuring angles and distances at the meridians in case no scales are available.

question: A VFR flight is planned from Landshut (EDML, 48°30.7'N 012°02.0"E) to Deggendorf (EDMW, 48°49.8'N 012°52.8"E), following the motorway A92. The height of the highest man-made obstacle in the vicinity of the route is:
explanation:
Refer to figure Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_13.
First, we will calculate how many feet the aircraft must descent. In order to do that, we have to convert the Flight Level (Pressure Altitude) to an altitude AMSL.

According to the question, we have ISA conditions ⇒ QNH = 1013 hPa

As a result, the Pressure Altitude is also an altitude AMSL, and therefore no pressure correction is required.

Thus, the height the aircraft has to descend is:   12500 ft - 3500 ft = 9000 ft

With a Rate of Descent of 500 ft/min, the time required to descent 9 000 ft is:  9000/500 min = 18 minutes

In order to calculate the distance covered during the descent, we must first calculate the Ground Speed.

Given that Track = 270º, TAS = 110 kt and W/V: 300º(T)/16 kt, with the help of a flight computer we get: Ground Speed = 96 kt

With a Ground Speed of 96 kt, the distance that can be covered in 18 minutes or 18/60 h is:

Distance = Ground Speed x Time = (96x18)/60 NM = 28.8 NM

This is the distance of the Top Descent (ToD) from the point abeam the airfield ALTÖTTING. However, the question asks for the distance of the Top of Descent (ToD) from the airfield MÜHLDORF. Therefore, we have to calculate the distance along the track from the point abeam the airfield ALTÖTTING to the airfield MÜHLDORF. This can be done as follows:
Use a compass for drawing circles and measure the distance along the track from the point abeam the airfield ALTÖTTING to the airfield MÜHLDORF.
Project this distance along a meridian. We can see that the distance along the track from the point abeam the airfield ALTÖTTING to the airfield MÜHLDORF is 6 NM. Remember that along a Meridian, 1 minute of latitude corresponds to 1 NM.
As a result, the distance of the Top of Descent from the airfield MÜHLDORF is: 28.8 NM + 6 NM = 34.8 NM ≈ 35 NM

Calculation of the Ground Speed with the help of a flight computer:

E6-B flight computer:
Move the slide until the ring falls on the arc marked 100.
Rotate the transparent (azimuth) disk to bring the True Wind Direction (300º) below the TRUE INDEX.
Starting from the ring, draw an UPWARD line, along the centerline, with a length equal to the Wind Velocity (16 kt). The line drawn represents the W/V vector.
Rotate the transparent (azimuth) disk to bring True Track (270º) below the TRUE INDEX. In this way, the Ground Speed/True Track vector is set on the centerline.
Move the slide until the end of the line falls over the arc that corresponds to the True Airspeed (110 kt). Thus, the triangle of velocities is now completed.
Determine the Ground Speed from the arc on which the ring falls. In this question, we can see that the ring falls on the arc that corresponds to 96. As a result, we have: Ground Speed = 96 kt
Æ Note: If you use an E6-B flight computer, you must have the following in mind:
If the question gives Track or Course, the W/V line must be drawn upwards.
If the question gives Heading, the W/V line must be drawn downwards.
CRP-5 flight computer:
Since TAS = 110 kt and Wind Velocity = 16 kt, we have:
The Lowest possible Ground Speed is: Ground Speed = TAS - Wind Velocity = 110 kt - 16 kt = 94 kt
The Highest possible Ground Speed is: Ground Speed = TAS + Wind Velocity = 110 kt + 16 kt = 126 kt
Since the Lowest possible Ground Speed and the Highest possible Ground Speed are both lower than 300 kt, we will use the Low Speed (L) side of the slide.
Move the slide to position the center dot on the speed arc marked 100.
Rotate the window disc to align the True Wind Direction (300º) with the TRUE HEADING index on the fixed scale.
Starting from the center dot, draw a DOWNWARD line, along the centerline, with a length equal to the Wind Velocity (16 kt).
Rotate the window disc to align the True Track (270º) with the TRUE HEADING index in the fixed scale.
Move the slide to position the center dot on the speed arc that corresponds to the True Airspeed (110 kt).
We can see that the end of the line is 5º to the Left of the centerline. Therefore, rotate the window disc until the True Track (270º) on the window disc is aligned with the line on the fixed scale that corresponds to 5º Left (Port) Drift Angle.
We can see that the end of the line is now 4º to the Left of the centerline. Therefore, rotate again the window disc until the True Track (270º) on the window disc is aligned with the line on the fixed scale that corresponds to 4º Left (Port) Drift Angle.
We can see that the end of the line still 4º to the Left of the centerline. This means that the Drift Angle on the fixed scale that is opposite of the True Track (270º) on the window disc is the same as the degrees the end of the line is to the Left of the centerline. Thus, the triangle of velocities is completed and the flight computer is ready for reading the Ground Speed.
Determine the Ground Speed from the speed arc which passes through the end of the line drawn. In this question, we can see that the 96 kt speed arc passes through the end of the line drawn. As a result, we have: Ground Speed = 96 kt
CR-3 flight computer:
Set the TAS index below 11 (11 represents the True Airspeed of 110 kt).
Rotate the green scale to bring the True Track (270º) above the TC index.
Find the 300º (Wind Direction) line on the green scale and on the point where this line intersects the  16 kt (Wind Velocity) circle, make a pencil mark. You have to interpolate in order to locate the 16 kt circle.
From the pencil mark, we can see that there is a Right crosswind component of 8 kt which, according to the flight computer's outer scale, corresponds to a crab angle of 4º.
Since there is a Right crosswind component, the True Heading is to the Right of the True Track. Thus, we have:
Wind Correction Angle = 4º Right
True Heading = True Track + 004º = 270º + 004º = 274º
Determine the Ground Speed. From the pencil mark, we can see that there a Headwind component of 14 kt. Thus, we have for the Ground Speed:
Ground Speed = True Airspeed - Headwind component = 110 kt - 14 kt = 96 kt
Æ Note 1: In the 6th step of the procedure (CR-3 flight computer), we used the True Airspeed, instead of the Effective True Airspeed, for the calculation of the Ground Speed. This is not wrong since the Wind Correction Angle is less than 10º. If the Wind Correction Angle is >= 10º, we must definitely use the  Effective True Airspeed. 

Æ Note 2: Definition of Wind Correction Angle: In simple words, Wind Correction Angle is how many degrees the Heading is to the left or to the right of the Track.
If the Heading is to the Left of the track, the Wind Correction Angle is Left.
If the Heading is to the Right of the track, the Wind Correction Angle is Right.
Æ note 3: Students reported that they have seen this question in 033 - flight planning and in 061 - general navigation exams, therefore we have added it to both subjects.

Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - ALICANTE - Charts 19-1, 19-2, 19-3

explanation:
The aircraft must fly at 9 220 ft or higher (7 220 + 2 000). But for us to fly at a true altitude that satisfies this condition, we must first correct the altitude for temperature and pressure errors.

Temperature

The ISA deviation is -10ºC. We know that the 4% rule states that we must correct 4% for every 10ºC of deviation:
4 × (10) × (9 220/1000) = 369 ft

Since the deviation is negative (colder than ISA), it means that the true altitude is lower than indicated, so we the value to true altitude making the indicated altitude equal to 9 589 ft.

Pressure

The difference from the standard mean sea level pressure is as follows: 1 013 - 990 = 23 hPa

And since the barometric lapse rate is 30 ft/hPa, the pressure correction is 23 x 30 = 690 ft
Since the pressure is lower than the standard we need to add to the indicated altitude (we rotate the altimeter knob from 990 to 1 013 since we're flying flight levels):

Pressure Altitude = 9 589 + 690 = 10 279 ft

Choosing flight level

You can disregard drift and variation since you choose your level solely based on the magnetic track.

Semi-circular cruising level system - Non RVSM flight levels - IFR

Magnetic Track 360° - 179° = ODD LEVEL
Magnetic Track 180° - 359° = EVEN LEVEL

As the Magnetic Track is 175º, an odd level shall be used, so the next usable IFR flight level up from 10 279 ft is FL110

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - NANTES - Charts 19-1, 19-2, 19-3
On a VFR flight, you need to transit around Nantes airport. Who shall you contact to inform about your intentions?
explanation:
Refer to figures 373 and Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_99.
First, we need to find LOGI (N47°29.7' E014°29.9') on the chart. We can notice that LOGI is located approximately in the middle of 4 different areas with different Grid Minimum Off-Route Altitudes (MORAs). The 4 different values are 10 600 ft, 10 100 ft, 10 300 ft and 10 800 ft. We have to choose the altitude which fulfills the obstacle clearance requirements for all 4 areas, which is the higher one (10 800 ft) and corresponds to the true altitude. So, this altitude must be corrected for the non-standard temperature and pressure before applying the semicircular altitude rule for VFR flights.

Temperature correction:
We will calculate the (minimum) indicated altitude with QNH set on the altimeter subscale that satisfies the obstacle clearance requirement for this flight. This can be done with the help of the following relationship:
 
True altitude = Indicated altitude (QNH) + Temperature correction
 
Solving this relationship with respect to Indicated altitude (QNH), we get:
 
Indicated altitude (QNH) = True altitude - Temperature correction   (1)
 
The temperature correction can be calculated with the help of the following relationship:
 
Temperature correction = 0.004 x ISA deviation x Height of the air column between the aircraft and the pressure datum
 
From the question we have: OAT = ISA - 15°C ⇒ ISA deviation = OAT - ISA = -15°C
 
Furthermore, since the elevation of the aerodrome, where QNH is measured, is 2 286 ft as shown on the chart, we have:
 
Height of the air column between the aircraft and the pressure datum = 10 800 ft - 2 286 ft = 8 514 ft
 
As a result, we have for the temperature correction:
 
Temperature correction = 0.004 x (-15) x 8 514 ft = -511 ft
 
Given that True altitude = 10 800 ft and Temperature correction = -511 ft, we have from the relationship (1):
 
Indicated altitude (QNH) = 10 800 ft - (-511 ft) = 11 311 ft
 
Pressure correction:
This is the (minimum) indicated altitude with QNH (1 018 hPa) set on the altimeter subscale which satisfies the obstacle clearance requirement for this flight. Since the standard pressure setting (1 013 hPa) is lower than the local QNH, changing from 1 018 hPa to 1 013 hPa, the pressure altitude will decrease by:
 
(1 018 hPa - 1 013 hPa) x 30 ft/hPa = 150 ft and will become:
 
Pressure altitude (1 013 hPa) = 11 311 ft - 150 ft = 11 161 ft
 
This is the (minimum) pressure altitude that satisfies the obstacle clearance requirement for this flight.
 
Since the question refers to a VFR flight and asks about the most suitable flight level without mentioning anything about magnetic heading, we have to apply the semicircular altitude rule for VFR flights which states that VFR flights follow even/odd flight levels + 500 ft. From the answer options given, we notice that only two answers fulfill this requirement, FL105 and FL115. Since the minimum pressure altitude is 11 161 ft, FL115 is the most suitable flight level.

Æ Note 1: The temperature correction is applied in the air column between the station where QFE is measured and converted to QNH (pressure datum) and the aircraft.
 
Æ Note 2: Pressure altitude is the indicated altitude with the standard pressure setting (1 013 hPa) set on the altimeter subscale.

explanation:
Refer to figure Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_97.
A protractor and a ruler can help you in the solution of the question. We can solve this question following the next procedure:
Locate on the chart the THALMÄSSING-WAIZENHOFEN (EDPW, N49°03.85' E011°12.57') airport and RODING VOR (RDG, N49°02.5' E012°31.5').
Use the ruler and draw a line which connects these two points. The line drawn represents the track of the flight from EDPW to RDG VOR. Extend the line after RDG VOR.
Set the center dot of the protractor on the center of the compass rose around RDG VOR and align the 000° of the protractor with the 000° of the compass rose. Remember that radials are referenced to Magnetic North and so does the compass rose around RDG VOR. We can measure clockwise an angle of approximately 89° between the 000° radial (Magnetic North) and the extended line after RDG VOR. Since this angle is measured with reference to the compass rose around RDG VOR, the measured angle gives the magnetic track from EDPW to RDG VOR. As a result, we have: magnetic track = 089°.
Æ note: You can also measure clockwise the angle between the E012°00' meridian, located between these points, and the line representing the track of the flight from EDPW to RDG VOR. This angle is approximately 92° and it's the true track of the flight, since meridians are referenced to True North. Then you can apply the local variation of 2.5°E (isogonal dashed line) using the mnemonic "Variation East, Magnetic Least" to find the magnetic track of the flight (89.5°, closest answer 89°).

explanation:
Refer to figure Enroute_ED-4_VFR_Expl_02.
QNH correction
The difference between the correct and incorrect QNH values is: 1010 - 1001 = 9 hPa. 9 hPa × 30 ft/hPa = 270 ft.

Minimum grid altitude
On the chart you can find a minimum grid altitude of 3 500 ft. It is indicated by the value "35" north east of Beilngries.

Temperature correction
ISA in 3 500 ft = 8 °C
OAT in 3 500 ft = 18 °C
ISA deviation = 18 °C - 8 °C = 10 °C
Temp. correction = 0.4 % × 10 = 4 %
Elevation of aerodrome: 1 210 ft
QNH altitude: 3 500 ft - 270 ft = 3 230 ft
Air column: 3 230 ft - 1 210 ft = 2 020 ft
Temp. correction: 4 % of 2 020 ft = 81 ft
True altitude: 3 230 ft + 81 ft = 3 311 ft (closest answer: 3 310 ft).

explanation:
ICAO Doc 8 168 Volume III
 
Chapter 4 – ALTIMETER CORRECTIONS
 
4.1 RESPONSIBILITY
 
4.1.1 Pilot’s responsibility
 
The pilot-in-command is responsible for the safety of the operation and the safety of the aeroplane and all of the persons on board during the flight time. This includes the responsibility for obstacle clearance, except when an IFR flight is being vectored.
 
Note – When an IFR flight is being vectored air traffic control (ATC) may assign minimum vectoring altitudes which are below Minimum Sector Altitude. Minimum vectoring altitudes provide obstacle clearance at all times until the aircraft reaches the point where the pilot will resume own navigation.
 
From the above extract, we can conclude that the pilot should follow the clearance of the controller to descend to 2 300 ft despite the fact that the cleared altitude is below the Minimum Sector Altitude (MSA) of 2 500 ft.

The pilot is preparing for a flight from Ajaccio (LFKJ) to Marina di Campo (LIRJ), routing along airways A9 and L146. Extract the information from the chart and complete the navigation plan. The total distance of the route and estimated flight time will be:
explanation:
Refer to figure 2226.

TAS:	440 kt
Leg distance:	300 NM
Course:	300°(M)
Variation:	20°E
Wind:	340°(T) / 40 kt

First, let's pass our course to True course: 300 + 20 (E) = 320º (T)

By drawing the situation, we can see that the wind is coming from our right and with Headwind component.

Let's find this Headwind component:

HW = 40 x cos (340 - 320) = 37.6  kt

TAS = 440 kt
GS = 440 - 37.6 = 402.4 kt

if we travel 402.4 NM in 60 minutes, 300 NM requires 44.8 minutes,

closest answer : 45 minutes

question:
Given:
 
TAS:	440 kt
Leg distance:	300 NM
Course:	300°(M)
Variation:	20°E
Wind:	340°(T) / 40 kt

Calculate the flight time for the given leg distance:
explanation:
First, we will calculate the outside air temperature (OAT) at FL390.

The ISA temperature at FL390 is -56.5°C ⇒ OAT = ISA - 10°C = -56.5°C - 10°C = -66.5°C

Now we can calculate the true airspeed (TAS) with the help of a flight computer:

E6-B flight computer:​
Rotate the inner dial until the MACH NO. INDEX arrow appears in the small window that is below the "DENSITY ALTITUDE" window or in the small window labeled "FOR TRUE AIRSPEED & DENSITY ALT." depending on the type of E6-B flight computer you use.
Align the outside air temperature (OAT) of -66.5°C with the MACH NO. INDEX arrow.
Without moving the flight computer scales, read the true airspeed on the outer scale that is opposite to the Mach number of 0.82 on the inner scale (the Mach number of 0.82 is represented by the second line to the right of the value 80 on the inner scale). We can read a TAS of (approximately) 460 kt.
CRP-5 flight computer:
Rotate the inner disc until the Mach No. Index arrow appears in the “AIR SPEED” window.
In the “AIR SPEED” window, align the Mach No. Index arrow with the outside air temperature (OAT) of -66.5°C.
Position the cursor on the Mach number of 0.82 on the inner scale (the Mach number of 0.82 is represented by the second long line to the right of the value 8 on the inner scale).
Read the true airspeed on the outer scale that is opposite to the Mach number of 0.82 on the inner scale. We can read a TAS of approximately 460 kt.
Æ Note: When the Mach No. Index is visible in the "AIR SPEED" window, the compressibility window is found to be blank. This is because the compressibility errors are already corrected for in the Mach meter mechanism and no correction is necessary on the computer.

CR-3 flight computer:
Rotate the inner disc until the double-ended MACH INDEX arrow appears in the small “PRESS. ALT.” window which is below the “DENSITY ALT.” window.
Align the outside air temperature (OAT) of -66.5°C with the double-ended MACH INDEX arrow.
Move the cursor on the Mach number of 0.82 on the inner scale (0.82 is represented by the second line to the right of the value 80 on the inner scale).
Read the true airspeed on the outer scale that is opposite to the Mach number of 0.82 on the inner scale. We can read a TAS of (approximately) 460 kt.
Æ Note: Depending on the flight computer you use, you might get a true airspeed that differs by 1-2 kt from the true airspeed (TAS) we calculated in our explanation.

We can also calculate the true airspeed (TAS) with the help of the Mach number formula:

Mach number = True airspeed/Local speed of sound

where Local speed of sound (LSS) = 38.95 x √(273 + OAT)

Solving the Mach number formula with respect to the true airspeed, we get:

True airspeed = Mach number x Local speed of sound   or

True airspeed = Mach number x 38.95 x √(273 + OAT)     (1)

Given that Mach number = 0.82 and OAT = -66.5°C, we get from relationship (1):

True airspeed = 0.82 x 38.95 x √(273 + (-66.5)) kt = 0.82 x 38.95 x √(273 - 66.5) kt =  0.82 x 38.95 x √(206.5) kt = 458.97 kt

Æ Note 1: For the solution of this question it is necessary to know that the ISA temperature above FL360 is -56.5°C.

Æ Note 2: The given wind component is irrelevant to the solution of the question.
 
Æ Note 3: The question text and the options have not been totally verified. So, if you encounter this question in your official exams, please provide us with any feedback that might help.

explanation: An LPV (Localizer Performance with Vertical guidance) approach is an RNP approach with vertical guidance (APV) and minimums which are typically lower than LNAV or LNAV/VAN minima. The angular lateral and vertical guidance of the LPV approach is provided by GNSS augmented by SBAS (such as EGNOS in Europe and WAAS in USA). Furthermore, the lateral guidance is equivalent to an ILS localizer and the vertical guidance is provided against a geometrical path in space rather than a barometric altitude. The parameters that define the geometry of the Final Approach Segment of an approach to LPV minima are contained into the FAS Data Block which is stored in the on-board navigation database. The LPV approach may downgrade to LNAV/VNAV minima if the vertical signal is lost or degraded.

question: What is an LPV approach?
options: A) An approach with vertical guidance based only on BaroVNAV.
B) An approach with LNAV/VNAV minima in reference with ground based facility.
C) An approach with LNAV minima that is flown using the CDFA technique.
D) An approach with vertical guidance based on GNSS augmented by space based facility.
correct_answer: D) An approach with vertical guidance based on GNSS augmented by space based facility.

explanation:
The two altitudes given serve different purposes — understanding the distinction is the key to answering this question.

MEA (Minimum Enroute Altitude) = 4 500 ft
The MEA guarantees two things simultaneously:
(1) Required obstacle clearance along the route segment
(2) Adequate reception of radio navigation aids and ATS communications

MOCA (Minimum Obstruction Clearance Altitude) = 1 500 ft
The MOCA guarantees only one thing:
(1) Required obstacle clearance within the protected dimensions of the route
It does NOT guarantee navaid or communications reception, except within 25 NM of the centreline.

Applying this to the question:
To operate safely on an IFR route, a pilot needs BOTH obstacle clearance AND navaid/comms reception. Only the MEA (4 500 ft) guarantees both. Therefore, the pilot should plan to fly at or above the MEA. Since MEA = 4 500 ft, planning at 5 000 ft satisfies the requirements while also complying to the IFR flight altitude rule.

B) Plan to fly between 2 000 ft and 4 000 ft in order to remain within the protected and authorised dimensions of the route segment.
C) Plan to fly at 5 000 ft or above to clear obstacles and to have adequate reception of radio navigation aids and communications.
D) Use the MEA value as the minimum altitude to achieve the required obstacle clearance for IFR flight as the MOCA value is for VFR flight.
correct_answer: C) Plan to fly at 5 000 ft or above to clear obstacles and to have adequate reception of radio navigation aids and communications.

question: What is the primary reason for studying SID & STAR charts before and during a flight?
options: A) To ensure the correct lateral and vertical profiles are flown.
B) To check for any changes to the published procedures.
C) To ensure that noise abatement procedures are observed.
D) In order to take account of any pre-planned flight deviations before the flight.
correct_answer: A) To ensure the correct lateral and vertical profiles are flown.
explanation:
RNAV approaches that make use of lateral and vertical guidance are often referred to as Approach Procedures with Vertical guidance (APV).
 
Baro APV approaches (baro-VNAV) use barometric inputs for vertical guidance which are flown by aircraft with VNAV functionality. These are normally referred to as LNAV/VNAV approaches.
 
SBAS APV approaches use the Satellite Based Augmentation Systems such as EGNOS in Europe, and WAAS in the USA (WAAS is the USA based implementation of SBAS, and are therefore the same thing). With SBAS APV, the vertical guidance is provided against a geometrical path in space, as opposed to a barometric altitude. The advantage of SBAS APV is that the vertical path is not reliant on a correct altimeter setting, unlike baro-VNAV. These are referred to as LPV approaches.

question: The legend on a chart indicates that the depicted procedure is categorised as an APV Baro or APV SBAS instrument approach procedure. The significance of it is that the approach is an approach procedure with vertical guidance, which is:

B) 3D precision approach down to minima published as DA or DH.
C) 2D non - precision approach down to minima published as LNAV or LP.
D) 2D non - precision approach down to minima published as MDA, LNAV or LP.
correct_answer: A) 3D approach down to minima published as LNAV/VNAV or LPV.

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - Amsterdam 10-2.
Which arrival route is correct when arriving via ROBIS?
explanation:
Refer to figure 448.
All you need to remember is CDMVT and the following relationships: 

Between TRUE and Magnetic:
Variation WEST, Magnetic BEST.
Variation EAST, Magnetic LEAST.

Between Magnetic and Compass:
Deviation West, Compass BEST.
Deviation EAST, Compass LEAST.
 
Find the Magnetic Heading (CDMVT): ​
Variation WEST, magnetic BEST. Variation is 6° WEST, so magnetic is 6° BEST (in other words, it is 6° higher than true heading):
042° + 6° = 048° Magnetic Heading.
Find the Compass Heading (CDMVT): ​
The deviation is -4°, and a minus sign is always WEST. So, Deviation WEST, Compass BEST means that Compass heading is going to be 4° higher than Magnetic heading:
048° + 4° = 052° Compass Heading.

Æ Note: Composition of designator

Selection of the letter shall be made from those listed hereunder:

a) A, B, G, R for routes which form part of the regional networks of ATS routes and are not area navigation routes;

b) L, M, N, P for area navigation routes which form part of the regional networks of ATS routes;

c) H, J, V, W for routes which do not form part of the regional networks of ATS routes and are not area navigation routes;

d) Q, T, Y, Z for area navigation routes which do not form part of the regional networks of ATS routes.
 
(Source: ICAO Annex 11 - Air Traffic Services)

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - Chart "E LO 1"

OVDAN: N57°53’, E000°18’
explanation:
Refer to figure Terminal_Aberdeen_10-1R_Expl_02.
Refer to ABERDEEN Chart 10-1R.
 
This chart shows the Radar Minimum Altitudes inside and outside the Designated Radar Minimum Altitude Area (the area whose limits are represented with bold lines) and the corresponding sectors.
 
First, locate Aberdeen airport. It is located in the middle of the Designated Radar Minimum Altitude Area.
 
Careful! On the chart, the courses of 180º, 270º, 360º and 90º correspond to inbound courses to the VOR, not the radials, According to the question, the aircraft is approaching Aberdeen airport from the South-West, this would be on an inbound course of 045º. From the chart, we can see that:
As the aircraft is flying outside the Designated Radar Minimum Altitude Area, it can maintain a minimum altitude of 4 100 ft. 
As the aircraft enters the Designated Radar Minimum Altitude Area, it can descend and maintain an altitude of 3 200 ft. 
At about 10 NM from Aberdeen airport, the aircraft can descend and maintain an altitude of 2 800 ft.
At about 5 NM from Aberdeen airport, the aircraft can descend and maintain an altitude of 2 300 ft.
So, among the given answers, the correct answer is: 3 200 ft to 2 800 ft to 2 300 ft
 
Æ Note 1: The aircraft is approaching Aberdeen airport, and not ABERDEEN VOR/DME, from the South-West. Therefore, the 1 700 ft sector, which is around ABERDEEN VOR/DME, must be excluded from the correct answer.
 
Æ Note 2: The student who provided us the feedback, mentioned that the aircraft was approaching Aberdeen airport from the SW. He didn’t mention any bearing. Thus, in the explanation of this question, we chose a flight path towards Aberdeen airport that enters the 3 200 ft sector. The answer”2 800 ft to 2 300 ft” can be also correct for an aircraft which approaches from SWW and does not enter the 3 200 ft sector. So, if you encountered this question in your exams, please let us know if the question and the answers appeared as here.

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - ABERDEEN - Chart 10-1R.
You are approaching Aberdeen airport from SW. What minimum radar altitudes are you passing?
options: A) 4 100 ft to 3 200 ft to 2 800 ft to 2 300 ft.
B) 2 500 ft to 2 300 ft to 1 700 ft.
D) 4 100 ft to 2 800 ft to 2 300 ft to 1 700 ft.
correct_answer: A) 4 100 ft to 3 200 ft to 2 800 ft to 2 300 ft.
explanation:
Refer to figure 373.
1) Pressure correction
QNH: 1 020 hPa
Standard: 1 013 hPa

1 020 - 1 013 = 7 hPa
Assuming 30 ft/hPa:
7 × 30 = 210 ft
Since QNH > 1 013 (high pressure), true altitude is higher than pressure altitude.

2) Temperature correction
At FL40, ISA temperature = 15 - (2 × 4) = 7°C
OAT: -7°C
Deviation: -7 - 7 = -14°C => ISA -14°C

Using 4% per 10°C rule: Correction = 4% × 1.4 × 4 000 = 224 ft
Cold temperature means true altitude is lower than indicated.

True altitude = 4 000 + 210 (QNH) - 224 (temp) = 3 986 ft
3 986 ft > 3 300 ft MOCA

Magnetic track: 182° (southbound/westbound: 180-359°)
IFR westbound requires even flight levels: FL40, FL60, FL80...

explanation:
Refer to figure Enroute_E(LO)5_Expl_02.
Based on the given coordinates, locate on the chart AKUTI (43°14'N, 008°56'E) and TORTU (43°23'N, 008°58'E).
 
According to the chart, these two points are connected with the ATS route A9 and for the part of the ATS route A9 between SUDAS and TORTU the following information in red colour is provided:
 
FL60
1 600T
 
The FL60 represents the Minimum En-route IFR Altitude (MEA) and the 1 600T represents the Minimum Obstacle Clearance Altitude (MOCA).
 
According to the definition of MOCA, the altitude of 1 600 ft meets the obstacle clearance requirements in the primary area for the entire segment between SUDAS and TORTU and implies an obstacle clarance of 1 000ft. See, if the obstacle clearance was 1 500ft, the minimum MOCA would be 4 500ft (3 000 + 1 500 = 4 500ft).
As a result, the highest obstacle within the primary area affecting that route segment should be 600 ft (= 1 600 ft - 1 000 ft) or less.
 
Æ Note 1: The short lines which appear as perpendicular to the route line at the end of the depicted ATS route segments indicate an altitude change.
 
Æ Note 2: From Jeppesen Glossary we have the following definitions:
 
MINIMUM ENROUTE IFR ALTITUDE (MEA) — The lowest published altitude between radio fixes that meets obstacle clearance requirements between those fixes and in many countries assures acceptable navigational signal coverage. The MEA applies to the entire width of the airway, segment, or route between the radio fixes defining the airway, segment, or route.
 
MINIMUM OBSTRUCTION CLEARANCE ALTITUDE (MOCA) — The lowest published altitude in effect between radio fixes on VOR airways, off airway routes, or route segments which meets obstacle clearance requirements for the entire route segment and in the USA assures acceptable navigational signal coverage only within 22NM of a VOR.
 
Æ Note 3: OBSTACLE CLEARANCE
The minimum obstacle clearance (MOC) value to be applied in the primary area for the en-route phase of an instrument flight rules (IFR) flight is 300 m (1 000 ft). In mountainous areas, this shall be increased depending on the variation in terrain elevation as follows:
 
Variation in terrain elevation	MOC
Between 900 m (3 000 ft) and 1 500 m (5 000 ft)	450 m (1 476 ft)
Greater than 1 500 m (5 000 ft)	600 m (1 969 ft)
 
(Source: ICAO Doc 8 168 Volume I)

question:
Refer to Jeppesen GSPRM Training Route Manual - Chart "E LO 5"
An aircraft is flying on airway A9, from AKUTI (43°14'N, 008°56'E) to TORTU (43°23'N, 008°58'E). The highest obstacle within primary area affecting that route segment should be ___ or less.
explanation:
In order to calculate the time from the destination aerodrome to the alternate aerodrome, we have to add the times of the following phases of flight:
Missed approach at the destination aerodrome.
Climb to cruising altitude.
Cruise from Top of Climb to Top of Descent.
Descent to the alternate aerodrome.
Approach and landing at the alternate aerodrome.
The question provides the time for each phase of flight apart from the Cruise phase. Thus, we have to calculate the duration of the Cruise phase.

We have TAS = 265 kt and a Headwind of 15 kt ⇒ Ground Speed = 265 kt - 15 kt = 250 kt.

With a Ground Speed of 250 kt (250 kt = 250 NM/hr = 250/60 NM/min), the cruise distance of 350 NM will be covered in:

Time = (350 x 60)/ 250 min = 84 min.

Thus, the time needed from the destination aerodrome to the alternate aerodrome is equal to:

6 min + 8 min + 84 min + 10 min + 5 min = 113 min.

Æ Note: The Ground Speed, the Distance and the Time are connected with the following relationship:
Ground Speed = Distance/Time.
Transforming this relationship with respect to Time, we get:  Time = Distance/Ground Speed.

question:
An aircraft is operated on a commercial flight. It has an approach climb gradient (ACG) capability of 3.7%. The three instrument approach procedures listed below are available at its destination. Which of these could be selected for the flight?
ILS/DME APP, minimum ACG required 3.0%
ILS/DME APP, minimum ACG required 3.7%
ILS/DME APP, minimum ACG required 4.2%
explanation:
The answer "Wind updates so the new ETA and remaining FUEL will be more accurate." is correct.
The GNSS/FMC combination continuously calculates and updates wind information during flight. This process works by the FMC using actual wind data for the current flight segment while using pilot-inputted forecast winds for segments beyond the next waypoint. To calculate actual winds, the system compares current position and ground speed from GNSS, heading data from the IRS (Inertial Reference System) or AHRS, and True Airspeed (TAS) from the Air Data Computer. As the aircraft progresses along its route, these continuous wind updates allow the FMC to recalculate and display more accurate ETAs (Estimated Time of Arrival) and provide more precise fuel remaining predictions. This is a significant advantage over aircraft without integrated GNSS/FMC systems, which would rely solely on manual calculations and forecasts without the benefit of real-time position, speed, and wind data integration.
 
The answer "Weather avoidance is easier." is incorrect.
​The GNSS/FMC system cannot detect weather conditions; that function is performed by the weather radar.
 
The answer “FMC can calculate wind without the input of other equipment” is incorrect.
In an aircraft with GNSS/FMC equipment, the wind is not calculated by the FMC but it uses either the actual wind derived from the inertial reference system (IRS) or the manually entered wind in order to calculate the ETA and the remaining fuel to the next waypoint. So, this is not an advantage of an aircraft with GNSS/FMC equipment.
 
The answer “FMC can create shortcut in function of the calculated wind” is incorrect.
With this option the creator of the question probably means that the FMC would suggest a new route or would suggest avoiding certain waypoints (i.e. flying direct to a waypoint down the rout) if it calculates that the tailwind on the shortcut sector is better than along the original route. However, the FMS does not do that and so it is not an advantage of an aircraft with GNSS/FMC equipment.
 
Æ Note 1: If you encounter this question in your official exams, please provide us any feedback that might help.

question: What is an advantage of using GNSS/FMC equipment in flight?
options: A) FMS can calculate wind without the input of other equipment.
C) Wind updates so the new ETA and remaining FUEL will be more accurate.
D) FMS can create shortcut in function of the calculated wind.
correct_answer: C) Wind updates so the new ETA and remaining FUEL will be more accurate.

question:
Given:
LP
LPV
LNAV
LNAV/VNAV
Approaches with vertical guidance (APV) are...
explanation:
Refer to figure 942.
The aircraft has to overfly an obstacle of 8 400 ft with a clearance of 2 000 ft.
 
This means that the minimum true altitude of the aircraft must be:  8 400 ft + 2 000 ft = 10 400 ft
 
First, we will calculate the minimum indicated altitude (QNH) which can be flown maintaining a minimum true altitude of 10 400 ft. This can be done with the help of the following relationship:
 
True altitude = Indicated altitude (QNH) + Temperature correction
 
Solving this relationship with respect to Indicated altitude (QNH) we get the following relationship:
 
Indicated altitude (QNH) = True altitude - Temperature correction
 
The temperature correction can be calculated with the help of the following formula:
 
Temperature correction = 0.004 x ISA deviation x height of the air column between the station and the aircraft
 
We have:
OAT = ISA - 15°C ⇒ ISA deviation = OAT - ISA = - 15°C
Height of the air column between the station and the aircraft = 10 400 ft
As a result, for the temperature correction we have:
 
Temperature correction = 0.004 x (-15) x 10 400 ft = -624 ft
 
Eventually, given that true altitude = 10 400 ft and temperature correction = -624 ft, we get:
 
Indicated altitude (QNH) = 10 400 ft - (-624 ft) = 10 400 ft + 624 ft = 11 024 ft
 
This is the indicated altitude with QNH = 1005 hPa set in the altimeter sub-scale. Since the standard pressure setting (1 013 hPa) is higher than the QNH, turning the altimeter setting from 1 005 hPa to 1 013 hPa, the indicated altitude will increase by: (1 013 - 1 005) hPa x 30 ft/hPa = 8 x 30 ft = 240 ft
 
As a result, the indicated altitude with 1 013 hPa set in the altimeter subscale will be: 11 024 ft + 240 ft = 11 264 ft
 
An indicated altitude of 11 264 ft is the minimum pressure altitude which can be flown to maintain a minimum true altitude of 10 400 ft.
 
The selection of the appropriate flight level is made based on the aircraft’s magnetic track. Since the aircraft’s magnetic track is 140°, the aircraft has to fly at an odd flight level. Thus, among the given answers, the minimum flight level at which an aircraft may fly in non-RVSM airspace maintaining a minimum true altitude of 10 400 ft, i.e. indicated altitude of 11 264 ft would be: FL130.
