explanation:
The question initially mentions that an alert has been generated by the FMS - the aircraft will arrive at the destination with 0.3 tonnes less fuel than required. Usually, FMS alerts are generated when the trip fuel and the contingency fuel have been consumed and the aircraft is using fuel from the fuel on board that corresponds to the sum of alternate fuel and final reserve fuel.
 
This alert is in full accordance with EASA AIR OPS (see below) regulations according to which the minimum fuel quantity that should be on board when arriving at the destination must not be less than the sum of the following fuel quantities:
Alternate fuel
Final reserve fuel.
 
CAT.OP.MPA.280 In-flight fuel management – aeroplanes
The operator shall establish a procedure to ensure that in-flight fuel checks and fuel management are carried out according to the following criteria.

(b) In-flight fuel management
 
(1) The flight shall be conducted so that the expected usable fuel remaining on arrival at the destination aerodrome is not less than
(i) the required alternate fuel plus final reserve fuel; or
(ii) the final reserve fuel if no alternate aerodrome is required
 
(2) If an in-flight fuel check shows that the expected usable fuel remaining on arrival at the destination aerodrome is less than:
(i) the required alternate fuel plus final reserve fuel, the commander shall take into account the traffic and the operational conditions prevailing at the destination aerodrome, at the destination alternate aerodrome and at any other adequate aerodrome in deciding whether to proceed to the destination aerodrome or to divert so as to perform a safe landing with not less than final reserve fuel; or
(ii) the final reserve fuel if no alternate aerodrome is required, the commander shall take appropriate action and proceed to an adequate aerodrome so as to perform a safe landing with not less than final reserve fuel.
 
(3) The commander shall declare an emergency when the calculated usable fuel on landing, at the nearest adequate aerodrome where a safe landing can be performed, is less than final reserve fuel
 
AMC1 CAT.OP.MPA.281 In-flight fuel management – helicopters
The operator should base in-flight fuel management procedures on the following criteria:
 
(b) In-flight fuel management
 
(1) If, as a result of an in-flight fuel check, the expected fuel remaining on arrival at the destination is less than the required alternate fuel plus final reserve fuel, the commander should:

                 (i) divert; or
                 (ii) replan the flight in accordance with SPA.HOFO.120 unless he/she considers it safer to continue to the destination.
 
(2)      At an onshore destination, when two suitable, separate touchdown and lift-off areas are available and the weather conditions at the destination comply with those specified for planning in CAT.OP.MPA.245(a)(2), the commander may permit alternate fuel to be used before landing at the destination.

question:
During cruise flight, the FMS generates an alert that you have 0.3 tonnes less fuel than required to reach your destination.
Under NORMAL circumstances, which fuel should remain upon arrival at your destination?
options: A) Alternate fuel plus final reserve fuel.
C) Alternate fuel plus contingency fuel plus final reserve fuel.
correct_answer: A) Alternate fuel plus final reserve fuel.
explanation:
Weight (kg) = Volume (L) × Density (kg/L)

In this case:
Total fuel volume (from wing tank measurements) × 0.79 kg/L = Fuel weight in kg

Excluding the remaining options:
Adding weights without considering density doesn't work because you're starting with volume measurements, not weight. You must convert volume to weight using density.
Ignoring density is incorrect because density is the conversion factor between volume and weight. Without it, you can't determine actual fuel weight.
You should use the provided density (0.79 kg/L) rather than an average. The specific density accounts for actual fuel characteristics under those conditions.

question: When calculating the weight of fuel with wing tanks and ACT 1, given a density of 0.79 kg/L, what method should be used?
options: A) Adding the weights without considering the density.
B) Multiplying the total volume (liters) by the provided density (0.79 kg/L) to obtain the weight.
C) Using an average density for fuel.
D) Ignoring the density information provided.
correct_answer: B) Multiplying the total volume (liters) by the provided density (0.79 kg/L) to obtain the weight.

explanation:
1 US Gallon = 3.7854 litres.
200 Gallons per hr = 757 litres per hr

The fuel flow rates of turbine engines are most commonly stated in terms of kg/hr or lb/hr (mass per hour) and not in litres or gallons (volume per hr). When the fuel flow is stated as "mass per hr" it is not influenced by the SG (Specific Gravity) of the fuel. This question is a bit tricky. What it is basically saying is that the fuel flow of this specific engine is 605 kg per hour (757 l/hr × 0.80) => this is an equivalent of 757 litres per hr (or 200 gallons per hr) if the SG of the fuel is 0.80. To obtain the fuel flow when the SG of the fuel is 0.75 we simply divide the 605 kg/hr value by the SG of 0.75 => 807.46 l/hr. Now to convert 806 litres to gallons we use the formula from the first line to obtain the result of 213 GPH.

If you understand the concept involved in specific gravity you can then use a very simple method instead of the above described one:  (200 GPH × 0.80) ÷ 0.75 => 213 GPH

explanation:
At 2 000 ft:
TAS: 120 kt
Wind: 20 kt headwind
Ground Speed = TAS - Headwind = 120 - 20 = 100 kt

Distance: 195 NM
Ground Speed: 100 kt
Time = Distance ÷ Ground Speed = 195 ÷ 100 = 1.95 hours

At 4 000 ft:
TAS: 120 kt
Wind: 10 kt tailwind
Ground Speed = TAS + Tailwind = 120 + 10 = 130 kt

Distance: 195 NM
Ground Speed: 130 kt
Time = Distance ÷ Ground Speed = 195 ÷ 130 = 1.5 hours

Now we need to enter the table and look for the fuel consumption figures:

At 2 000 ft (18'):
Fuel rate: 3.2 USG/h
Total fuel: 3.2 × 1.95 = 6.24 USG

At 4 000 ft (22'):
Fuel rate: 7.9 USG/h
Total fuel: 7.9 × 1.5 = 11.85 USG

Difference: 11.85 - 6.24 ≈ 5.5 USG increase

options: A) Multiply NGM by true airspeed (TAS) and divide by groundspeed (GS).
B) Multiply NGM by groundspeed (GS) and divide by true airspeed (TAS).
C) Divide NGM by groundspeed (GS) and divide by true airspeed (TAS).

explanation:
Refer to figure 445.
Detailed explanation
Make sure to use the correct table (23 in. Hg @ 2 300 RPM). Since the outside air temperature (OAT) at 8 000 ft is 13 °C we know that the ISA deviation is approx. +14 °C. Calculation:
ISA at MSL = 15 °C
ISA at 8 000 ft = -2 °C × 8 + 15 °C = -1 °C
Actual temperature at 8 000 ft (OAT) = 13 °C, therefore, it is 14 °C warmer than ISA ⇒ ISA +14 °C
The table shows values for ISA +0 °C and ISA +20 °C:
Fuel Flow (PPH) for ISA+0 at 8 000 ft is 71.1 lb/hr
Fuel Flow (PPH) for ISA+20 at 8 000 ft is 68.5 lb/hr
...but no values for ISA +14 °C, therefore we have to interpolate.

Add all information into a table or in some similar format so it is easier to recognize in which direction (up or down) you have to calculate. After you have added those values to your table, you can, step by step, calculate the results. Our calculations are shown in italic but first we have to calculate the change in PPH per 1 °C change:
71.1 - 68.5 = 2.6 = total difference (PPH)
20 - 0 = 20 = total difference (°C)
2.6 / 20 = 0.13 PPH/°C
Now, find the differences in temperature (14 °C and 6 °C), then, calculate PPHs for those differences (1.82 and 0.78), then add or subtract the results to get the final result (69.28 PPH).
 	ISA Dev	Fuel (PPH)	 
 	0	71.1	 
difference:	14	1.82	⇔ 0.13 ×  14 = 1.82
 	14	69.28	ca. 69.3 PPH
difference:	 6	0.78	⇔ 0.13 ×  6 = 0.78
 	20	68.5	 
Caution:	values
increasing
(0 ⇒ 20)	values
decreasing
(71.1 ⇒ 68.5)	(from top to bottom,
values change in opposite sense)
Note: PPH = pound per hour = lb/hr

Speed calculation
KTAS for ISA+0 at 8 000 ft is 160 kt
KTAS for ISA+20 at 8 000 ft is 160 kt
⇒ no interpolation necessary


Shortcut
Pick out the values from the table and find the average or mean value, i.g. for +10 °C:
0 °C	71.1 PPH
+10 °C	69.8 PPH
+20 °C	68.5 PPH
OAT 13 °C in 8 000 ft, which is given by the question = ISA +14 °C. Therefore the result must be between 69.8 PPH and 68.5 PPH. Since TAS is 160 kt for both, you can simply choose the correct answer, since only one answer is below 71.1 PPH.
Caution: It is good to know how you can make a shortcut because you can safe time, especially when time runs out, but we strongly recommend to learn the detailed and accurate way as well. Firstly, you simply have to know and understand this stuff, secondly, questions and values can be different in real exams and some CAAs use type in (TEXT) functions where you have to type in the correct answer without any references.

Given: True airspeed: 90 kt Air distance: 240 NAM Headwind: 30 kt Calculate nautical ground miles:
explanation:
Refer to figure 1756.
We can calculate the air distance with the help of the following formula:
 
Air distance = (TAS/GS) x Ground distance
 
The question gives the TAS (145 kt) and the Ground distance (57 NM). Thus, we will calculate the Ground speed.
 
In order to calculate the Ground speed, the Wind direction and the course must refer to the same North, True or Magnetic. From the given data, we can see that the Wind direction is True while the course is Magnetic (M). Thus, using the given variation, we have to convert either the Wind direction to Magnetic or the course to True. In this explanation, we will convert the course from Magnetic to True:
 
Variation = 15° W ⇒ Magnetic North is 15° West of True North ⇒ True course = Magnetic course - 015° = 205° - 015° = 190°
 
Given that TAS = 145 kt, True course = 190° and W/V: 240° (T)/30 kt, with the help of the triangle of velocities or using a flight computer we get: Ground speed = 124 kt
 
Thus, from the relationship above, we have for the air distance:
 
Air distance = (145/124) x 57 NM = 66.65 NM (closest answer 66 NM)
 
Calculation of the Ground speed
 
With the triangle of velocities (calculation method):
Using a ruler and a protractor, draw the triangle of velocities. Remember that the tip of the True airspeed/True heading vector must be on the tail on the W/V vector. Consider: AB: Ground speed/True course vector, AC: True airspeed/True heading vector, BC: W/V vector.
Starting from point B, extend the Ground speed/True course vector (AB).
Starting from point C, draw a straight line which is at right angles to the extension of Ground speed/True course vector. Consider D the point of intersection of this line with the Ground speed/True course vector. 
Since True course = 190° and True Wind direction = 240°, we have: angle ABC = 130° and angle CBD = 50°

In the triangle BCD we have:
sin (CBD) = Crosswind Component/Wind velocity ⇒ Crosswind component = Wind velocity x sin (CBD) = 30 x sin (50°) kt = 22.98 kt
cos (CBD) = Headwind Component/Wind velocity ⇒ Headwind component = Wind velocity x cos (CBD) = 30 x cos (50°) kt = 19.28 kt
Consider a the angle between the Ground speed/True course vector (AB) and the True airspeed/True heading vector (AB)
 
In the triangle ACD we have:  
sin (a) = Crosswind Component (CD)/ True airspeed = 22.98/145 ⇒ a = 9.12°
cos (a) = AD/True airspeed ⇒ AD = True airspeed x cos (a) = 145 x cos (9.12°) kt = 143.17 kt (this is the Effective True airspeed).
Eventually, for the Ground speed, we have:

Ground speed = Effective True airspeed - Headwind Component = 143.17 kt - 19.28 kt = 123.89 kt
 
With flight computers:
 
E6-B flight computer:
Move the slide until the ring (center) falls over the 100 kt speed arc.
Rotate the transparent (azimuth) disc until the True Wind direction (240°) is under the TRUE INDEX.
Starting from the ring (center), draw an upward line, along the centerline, with a length equal to the Wind Velocity (30 kt). This line represents the W/V vector.
Rotate the transparent (azimuth) disc until the True course (190°) is under the TRUE INDEX. In this way, the Ground speed/True course vector is set on the centerline.
Move the slide until the end of the line drawn falls on the True airspeed arc of 145 kt. Thus, the triangle of velocities is now completed.
Determine the Ground speed from the speed arc which is under the ring (center). In this question we can see that the 124 kt speed arc is under the ring (center). As a result, we have: Ground speed = 124 kt
Æ Note: If you use an E6-B flight computer, you must the following in your mind:
If the question gives track or course, the W/V line must be drawn upwards.
If the question gives heading, the W/V must be drawn downwards.
CRP-5 flight computer:
Since TAS = 145 kt and Wind velocity = 30 kt, we have:
The lowest possible Ground speed is: Ground speed = TAS - Wind velocity = 145 kt - 30 kt = 115 kt
The highest possible Ground speed is: Ground speed = TAS + Wind velocity = 145 kt + 30 kt = 175 kt
Since the lowest possible Ground speed and the highest possible Ground speed are both lower than 300 kt, we will use the Low speed (L) side of the slide.
Move the slide to position the center dot on the 100 kt speed arc.
Rotate the window disc to align the True Wind direction (240°) with the TRUE HEADING index on the fixed scale.
Starting from the center dot, draw a downward line, along the centerline, with a length equal to the Wind velocity (30 kt).
Rotate the window disc to align the True course (190°) with the TRUE HEADING index on the fixed scale.
Move the slide to position the center dot on the True airspeed arc of 145 kt.
We can see that the end of the line is 10° the left of centerline. Therefore, rotate the window disc to align the True course (190°) on the window disc with the line on the fixed scale that corresponds to a 10° left drift angle.
We can see now that the end of the line is 9° to the left of the centerline. Therefore, rotate again the window disc to align the True course (190°) on the window disc with the line on the fixed scale that corresponds to 9° left drift angle.
We can see now that the end of the line is still 9° to the left of the centerline. This means that the drift angle on the fixed scale that is opposite of the True course (190°) on the window disc is the same as the degrees the end of the line is to the left of the centerline. Thus, the triangle of velocities is now completed and the flight computer is ready for reading the Ground speed.
Determine the Ground speed from the speed arc that passes below the end of the line drawn. In this question we can see that the speed arc that passes below the end of the line corresponds to approximately 124 kt. As a result, we have: Ground speed = 124 kt
Æ Note: If you use a CRP-5 flight computer, you can also follow the procedure described for the E6-B flight computer. The procedure described above is according to the CRP-5 flight computer manual.
 
CR-3 flight computer:
Set the TAS index below 14.5 (14.5 represents the TAS of 145 kt).
Rotate the green scale until the True course (190°) is above the TC index.
Find the 240° (Wind direction) line on the green scale and on the point where this line intersects the 30 kt (Wind velocity) green circle make a pencil mark.
From the pencil mark we can see that there is a right crosswind component of 23 kt which, according to the flight computer’s outer scale, corresponds to a crab angle of 9°.
Since there is a right crosswind component, the True heading is 9° right of the True course. Thus, we have:
Wind correction angle (WCA) = 9° right or +9°
Determine the Ground speed. From the pencil mark we can see that there is a headwind component of 19 kt. Thus, using the Effective True airspeed, we have for the Ground speed:
Ground speed = Effective True airspeed - Headwind component = TAS x cos (WCA) - headwind component = 145 x cos (9°) kt - 19 kt = 124.21 kt
Æ Note: In the 6th step of the procedure (CR-3 flight computer) we used the Effective True airspeed for the calculation of the Ground speed. Since the WCA is lower than 10° we can also use the True airspeed. In this case, the Ground speed will be calculated by subtracting the headwind component directly from the True airspeed (Ground speed = TAS - headwind component = 145 kt - 19 kt = 126 kt).

question:

Given:

What is the total mass of the fuel in kilograms for an aircraft with 83 US Gallons of fuel on board, if the specific gravity of the fuel is given as 6 lbs/US Gallon?

(Assume 1 kg = 2.205 lb)
explanation:
To determine the available flight time, we must first calculate the amount of fuel that can be used en route. The total usable fuel is 1 800 lbs, but the aircraft must land with at least 350 lbs remaining. Therefore, the fuel available for flight is:
1800 lbs (total) − 350 lbs (required on landing) = 1450 lbs available for cruise
The aircraft consumes fuel at a constant rate of 500 lbs per hour. To find the time available, divide the usable fuel by the hourly fuel flow:
1450 lbs ÷ 500 lbs/hour = 2.9 hours
Convert 0.9 hours into minutes:
0.9 × 60 = 54 minutes
So, the total available time to reach the destination is 2 hours and 54 minutes.

explanation:
1) Basic mission fuel: 400 kg

2) Calculate the 45-minute extra fuel
45 min = 0.75 hours
Extra fuel = 200 × 0.75 = 150 kg

3) Calculate total additional weight
Parcel: 300 kg
Extra fuel: 150 kg
Total additional weight: 450 kg

4) Calculate weight penalty
Effect: 10 kg extra fuel per 100 kg additional weight
Weight penalty = (450 ÷ 100) × 10 = 45 kg

Take-off fuel required = standard fuel + extra fuel + penalty = 400 + 150 + 45 = 595 kg

explanation:
1) Basic mission fuel: 400 kg

2) Calculate the 45-minute extra fuel
45 min = 0.75 hours
Extra fuel = 200 × 0.75 = 150 kg

3) Calculate total additional weight
Parcel: 300 kg
Extra fuel: 150 kg
Total additional weight: 450 kg

4) Calculate weight penalty
Effect: 10 kg extra fuel per 100 kg additional weight
Weight penalty = (450 ÷ 100) × 10 = 45 kg

Take-off fuel required = standard fuel + extra fuel + penalty = 400 + 150 + 45 = 595 kg

Trip fuel:	3 780 kg

Calculate the minimum required fuel on board for a commercial flight with a jet and a fuel flow of 2 232 kg/hr at 1 500 ft above the destination.
explanation:
At the start of the take-off, the mass of the aircraft consists of Dry Operating Mass (DOM) + Traffic Load (TL) + Fuel (Trip + Contingency + Alternate + Final reserve). As the aircraft reaches destination it will have used the Trip fuel => therefore the fuel remaining in the tanks will be Contingency + Alternate + Final Reserve. If the aircraft diverts to the alternate it will use the Alternate fuel for this trip and upon landing at alternate the only fuel remaining in the tanks will be the Contingency + Final Reserve (+ any Extra fuel that commander chose to carry in addition to the minimum required amount). From the above we can easily calculate the landing mass at alternate in this way: DOM (33 000 kg) + TL (8 110 kg) + Contingency (102 kg) + Final reserve fuel (983 kg) = 42 195 kg.

Æ note: There is often a lot of confusion about the contingency fuel - whether it is considered to have been used by the time the aircraft reached the destination or whether it remains in the tanks. If the flight goes as planned, the contingency fuel is not used and remains in the tanks upon reaching the destination. The purpose of the contingency fuel is to account for example for minor route alterations or basically if anything does not go exactly as planned (e.g. a deviation around a thunderstorm; stronger headwind than expected; lower cruising altitude than expected, etc...). Most of the questions ask about the planned masses - in this case you plan on not using the contingency fuel and the fuel on board when reaching the destination = Contingency + Alternate + Final reserve + any Extra fuel.

Æ note update: at the beginning of the year 2018 a student reported that this question appeared in his exam and a note was added to the contingency fuel information: "not consumed". It seems EASA is updating such questions to avoid unnecessary confusion. Please let us know if more questions with such info pop up. Thank you.

For the ground operations, the flight crew must  consider the following amount of fuel:
explanation:
Refer to figure 432.
Enter the table on the line where you see the value of "204" (204 thousand) in the first column. Find the box where the row intersects a value of "4" and "6" in the top column (= 4 and 6 hundred) - we have to interpolate between these two values since there is no value for 500 => find results of 7 436 NM and 7 452 NM => by interpolating these two values ((7 436 + 7 452) ÷ 2) we get a result of 7 444 NM. On the same line, in the last column, we can find the TAS of 471 kt. Now we need to fly a distance of 2 000 NM (Ground Miles) - we have to convert it to NAM (Air Miles) using a formula NAM = NM x TAS ÷ GS => 2 000 x 471 kt ÷ 401 = 2 349 NAM. Deduct this figure from the total distance that we can fly with a mass of 204 500 kg => 7 444 - 2 349 = 5 095 NM.

Now reverse the steps above - in the table, find a box where you see the distance of 5 095 NM and then read the corresponding time and mass in thousands in the first column on the left and in hundreds in the first row at the top. Since there is no distance of 5 095 we have to interpolate between 5 080 NM and 5 098 NM (masses of 176 600 and 176 800) => by interpolation you find out that for a distance of 5 095 NM the corresponding mass is 176 767 kg. Therefore, to the fly distance of 2 000 NGM, the fuel required will be the difference of 204 500 kg and 176 767 kg => 27 733 kg.

The last step now is to make adjustments for the use of total anti-ice. See the notes at the bottom of the table - Total anti-ice "ON" = +6% of fuel burn => 27 733 x 1.06 = 29 396 kg.

explanation:
According to EASA regulation, when a flight is conducted with a required destination alternate, the flight must be planned and managed so that upon landing at the destination aerodrome, the usable fuel remaining is not less than the required alternate fuel plus the final reserve fuel. The contingency fuel is not part of this minimum because it is intended to compensate for unforeseen factors that could affect the fuel consumption on the way to the destination, such as deviations from the planned route, unexpected weather, or ATC delays. Since these factors may or may not occur, the contingency fuel might already have been used during the flight. Therefore, when determining the absolute minimum fuel that must remain upon landing, EASA excludes contingency fuel from the calculation and only requires that the alternate fuel and final reserve fuel remain intact to guarantee safe continuation to the alternate aerodrome if necessary and to provide the legally required final reserve at the end of the flight.

Regulation for aeroplanes:
EASA AIR OPS
ED Decision 2022/005/R
AMC1 CAT.OP.MPA.185(a) Fuel/energy scheme — in-flight fuel/energy management policy — aeroplanes
[...]
(b) In-flight fuel management
(1) The flight should be conducted to ensure that the usable fuel expected to remain upon landing at the destination aerodrome is not less than:
(i) the required alternate fuel plus the FRF; or
(ii) the FRF if no alternate aerodrome is required.
[...]
 
Regulation for helicopters:
EASA AIR OPS
ED Decision 2022/005/R
AMC1 CAT.OP.MPA.195 Fuel/energy scheme — in-flight fuel/energy management policy — helicopters
ENSURING A SAFE LANDING FOR COMPLEX MOTOR-POWERED HELICOPTERS IN OTHER THAN LOCAL OPERATIONS
[...]
(b) in-flight fuel management:
(1) if an in-flight fuel check shows that the usable fuel that is expected to remain upon landing at the destination is less than the required alternate fuel plus the FRF, the commander should:
(i) divert; or
(ii) replan the flight in accordance with point SPA.HOFO.120(b)(1) unless the commander considers it safer to proceed to the destination; and
[…]

question:
Refer to figure 033-26.
A turbojet aeroplane, weighing 200 000 kg, cruises at the optimum level at M 0.84 (ISA, CG = 37 %, Total Anti Ice ON). A headwind of 30 kt is experienced and, after a distance of 500 NM, severe icing is encountered and this requires an immediate descent. The aeroplane mass at start of descent is:
explanation:
The maximum amount of discretionary fuel that can be carried is equal to the lowest of the following fuel quantities:
 
Maximum take-off mass (MTOM) - Actual take-off mass = MTOM - (Dry Operating mass + Traffic load + Planned take-off fuel) = 233 000 kg - (122 550 kg + 43 217 kg + 51 616kg) = 233 000 kg - 217 383 kg = 15 617 kg
 
Maximum landing mass (MLM) - Actual landing mass = MLM - (Actual take-off mass - trip fuel) = MLM - (Dry operating mass + Traffic load + Planned take-off fuel - Trip fuel) = 182 000 kg - (122 550 kg + 43 217 kg + 51 616kg - 45 854 kg) = 182 000 kg - 171 529 kg = 10 471 kg
 
Tank capacity - Block fuel = Tank capacity - (Planned take-off fuel + taxi fuel) = 110 000 kg - (51 616 kg + 300 kg) = 110 000 kg - 51 916 kg = 58 084 kg
 
We can see that the lowest calculated fuel quantity is 10 471 kg, i.e. the maximum amount of discretionary fuel is limited by the maximum landing mass (MLM).
 
Based on these calculations a discretionary amount of 10 471 kg can be loaded before departure.  However as the aircraft will now be heavier the Trip Fuel will be greater.  This additional Trip Fuel is calculated with the quoted Fuel Penalty as shown below.
 
Increased Trip Fuel = 394 x (10 471 / 1 000) = 4 125 kg.
 
Discretionary Fuel that can be loaded before departure = 10 471 + 4 125 = 14 597 kg.
 
This discretionary amount must not exceed the MTOM limitation or tank capacity, and this value complies.
 
Æ Note 1: Discretionary fuel was implemented in the latest EASA AIR OPS regulations (Regulation (EU) 2 021/1296) and it is the fuel which is added at the discretion of the commander - in the old EASA AIR OPS regulations, the fuel which was added at the discretion of the commander was called extra fuel. In the latest regulations, extra fuel is the fuel for anticipated delays or specific operational constraints. EASA has not changed the calculations of the questions but only the wording. Since not all CAAs switch to the latest EASA ECQB at once, it is possible that some CAAs still show the older versions of the questions. Therefore we decided to keep old and new information within the questions and explanations.

Æ Note 2: General information about the procedure:
We are sure that the following procedure is used by EASA because it was confirmed by a flight instructor who was in contact with EASA. Anyway, please let us know if you make different experiences in your official exams.

The maximum discretionary amount of fuel that can be carried is limited either by MTOM, MLM or Tank Capacity:
If MTOM is the limit (giving X kgs or lb of discretionary fuel) then as long as X + TOF does not exceed tank capacity, the answer is X.
If MLM is the limit (giving Y kgs or lb of discretionary fuel) then more than Y can be uplifted as discretionary fuel means a heavier aircraft which means more trip fuel.  This increase in trip fuel is described as a "fuel penalty (normally given as Z kgs or lb per 1 000 kgs or lb)" in the question.  This increase in trip fuel = (MLM discretionary / 1 000) x Z.  This increase in trip fuel will be burned en-route so arrival at the destination will be with the MLM discretionary intact.  The discretionary fuel that can be uploaded = MLM discretionary + increased trip fuel (MLM discretionary / 1 000) x Z.  This should be checked against MTOM discretionary and tank capacity.
The questions that state a fuel penalty and require an answer which is limited by MLM are currently excluded from the examinations by EASA and should not be asked as the solution process originally agreed upon has been changed - leading to confusion. If such question appears in your official exam, you can appeal (note from 2019-10-22: a student from Spain confirmed that such question appeared in his exam).

explanation: Carrying extra fuel makes the aircraft heavier, and a heavier aircraft must generate more lift, which increases induced drag and requires more thrust throughout the flight. That extra thrust translates directly into extra fuel flow. In other words, some portion of the 5 000 kg must be burned simply to carry the 5 000 kg—this is the “fuel‑to‑carry‑fuel” penalty. Because of this penalty, you will land with less than the full 5 000 kg preserved for the next leg. The exact amount lost depends on aircraft type, sector length, CI, winds, and altitude profile, but the direction is unambiguous: the additional weight increases trip fuel, so the net fuel available on arrival is less than what was uplifted as “extra”. A simple way to visualize it is that each kilogram you add imposes a small burn cost over the leg; over the whole flight, those small costs add up to hundreds of kilograms or more, so you cannot arrive with the full 5 000 kg intact. This is precisely why flight planning accounts for a fuel‑carriage penalty whenever discretionary or tankering fuel is considered.

explanation:
The total amount of fuel required for this commercial flight, should include the following fuel quantities:

Taxi fuel, Trip fuel, Contingency fuel, Final reserve fuel, Alternate fuel.

We have for each fuel quantity:

Taxi fuel = 345 kg 

Trip fuel = 72 340 kg

Contingency = MAX (5 min holding AAL or 5% trip) 

Contingency fuel =  5 min holding AAL : if we consume 5 592 in 60 minutes, 5 minutes consume 466 kg (5 x 5 592 / 60)
                                  or 5 % trip fuel = 0.05 x 72 340 = 3 617 kg

Final reserve fuel ( Final,jet = 30 mins hold 1 500 ft AAL ) = 5 592 / 2 = 2 796 kg

Alternate fuel = 4 589 kg

The RAMP fuel will therefore be 345 + 72 340 + 3 617 + 2 796 + 4 589 = 83 687 kg

explanation:
1. Calculate the temperature deviation from ISA
At FL370 the table's standard TAT is −32 °C. Actual TAT is −42 °C, so the deviation is −10 °C (colder than standard). From the notes on top, this requires:
FF: −3% per 10 °C → −3%
TAS: −1 kt per 1 °C → −10 kt

2. Interpolate for 52 500 kg at FL370
52 500 kg is the midpoint between the 55 000 kg and 50 000 kg columns:
FF/ENG = (1 006 + 921) / 2 = 963.5 kg/h per engine
TAS = (439 + 425) / 2 = 432 kt

3. Apply the temperature corrections calculated above
FF/ENG = 963.5 × 0.97 = 934.6 kg/h per engine
Total FF (two engines) = 2 × 934.6 = 1 869.2 kg/h
TAS = 432 − 10 = 422 kt

4. Time and trip fuel
With no wind information given, GS = TAS, so 150 NGM is covered in:
Time = 150 / 422 = 0.3555 h
Trip fuel = 1 869.2 × 0.3555 ≈ 664 kg

explanation:
EASA AIR OPS
Annex I Definitions for terms used in Annexes II to VIII
‘contingency fuel/energy’ means the fuel/energy required to compensate for unforeseen factors that could have an influence on the fuel/energy consumption to the destination aerodrome;

Contingency fuel, as defined by EASA AIR OPS Regulation, is the additional fuel carried by an aircraft to account for unforeseen factors that might increase fuel consumption to the destination aerodrome (not alternate aerodrome). This includes variations in meteorological conditions, deviations from planned routes, etc. Contingency fuel is not part of the trip fuel, but is calculated based on a percentage of it.

question: What is the reason of carrying contingency fuel?
options: A) To provide a reserve for foreseen circumstances occurring between departure and alternate.
B) To provide a reserve for foreseen circumstances occurring between departure and destination.
C) To provide a reserve for unforeseen circumstances occurring between departure and destination.
D) To provide a reserve for unforeseen circumstances occurring between departure and alternate.
correct_answer: C) To provide a reserve for unforeseen circumstances occurring between departure and destination.

question: How is the mass of the aircraft at destination assumed?

B) Zero fuel mass, alternate, final reserve fuel, contingency.
C) Zero fuel mass, final reserve fuel, contingency.
D) Zero fuel mass, alternate, contingency.
correct_answer: A) Zero fuel mass, alternate, final reserve fuel.
explanation:
Refer to figures 210 and 1004.
At the start of the take-off, the mass of the aircraft consists of Dry Operating Mass (DOM) + Traffic Load (TL) + Take-off Fuel (Trip fuel + Contingency + Alternate + Final reserve). As the aircraft reaches destination it will have used the Trip fuel => therefore the fuel remaining in the tanks will be Contingency + Alternate + Final Reserve.

explanation:
Refer to figure 347.
Enter chart in the bottom section - draw a vertical line starting from a value of 2 000 NM all the way up (assume zero wind) until you intersect a line representing FL300 in the upper section (line "29 and ABOVE"). From this point, track horizontally to the left scale (disregard ISA correction) to read a value of approx. 04 hrs 48 min. Now repeat the same, but adjust for 25 kt headwind => draw a line from 2 000 NM to wind ref line, then diagonally slightly up and to the right to intersect a 25 kt headwind line, then continue all the way up to FL300 ref line, then horizontally to the left (again disregard ISA) to read a result of approx. 05 h 12 min. 04:48 equals to 288 minutes; 05:12 equals to 312 minutes. 288 minutes = 100%, 312 minutes = approx. 108%. Therefore a 25 kt headwind increases the trip time by approx. 8%.

A commercial flight requires two destination alternates, due to significant weather condition close to the destination. The following data are given.

Average cruise fuel flow: 46 kg/min  
Fuel for missed approach: 400 kg  

Flight time from missed approach altitude to destination alternate 1: 54 min  
Flight time from missed approach altitude to destination alternate 2: 44 min

The required alternate fuel is:

Given the following information:
 
Maximum Take-off Mass (MTOM):	2 100 lb
Zero Fuel Mass (ZFM):	1 720 lb
Maximum Landing Mass (MLM):	1 900 lb
Fuel tank capacity:	54.5 USG
Fuel at take-off:	32 USG
Trip fuel:	25 USG
Specific density:	0.72
 
How much discretionary fuel (formerly called extra fuel) can be taken on?
explanation:
Refer to figure 1238.
According to EASA AIR OPS regulations, the total amount of fuel required for a Commercial Air Transport flight in a Medium Range Transport (MRJT) aeroplane is the sum of the following fuel quantities:
 
Taxi fuel/energy:
The total taxi fuel/energy comprises the fuel required for taxiing at the departure aerodrome prior to take-off and the APU consumption.
 
Since the time of APU usage is 15 minutes and the APU fuel flow is 115 kg/h or 115/60 kg/min, we have:
 
APU consumption = (15 min) x (115/60 kg/min) = 28.75 kg
 
Since the planning taxi time at the departure aerodrome is 15 minutes and the taxi fuel flow is 11 kg/min, we have:
 
Taxi fuel = (15 minutes) x (11 kg/min) = 165 kg
 
As a result, we have: Total taxi fuel = 165 kg + 28.75 kg = 193.75 kg
 
Æ Note: The taxi time at the destination is not taken into account for the calculation of the taxi fuel/energy and it is probably given to confuse you.
 
Trip fuel/energy:
Since the average fuel flow for the cruise phase (2 878 kg/h) is provided, we will calculate the flight time of the trip.
 
Given TAS = 440 kt and average headwind component = 16 kt, we have: Ground speed (GS) = 440 kt - 16 kt = 424 kt
 
With a GS of 424 kt, the time required to cover the trip distance of 315 NM is:
 
Time = Distance/Ground speed = (315 NM)/(425 NM/h) = 315/424 h
 
Since the average fuel flow for the cruise phase is 2 878 kg/h, the trip fuel required is:
 
Trip fuel = (315/424 h) x (2 878 kg/h) = (315x2878)/424 kg = 2 138.14 kg
 
Contingency fuel/energy:
According to the question, the contingency fuel is the amount of fuel required to fly for 5 minutes at holding speed. 
 
Since the holding fuel flow is 2 200 kg/hr or 2 200/60 kg/min, we have:
 
Contingency fuel = (5 min) x (2 200/60 kg/min) = (5x2200)/60 kg = 183.33 kg
 
Additional fuel/energy:
According to the latest EASA AIR OPS regulations, since the flight is operated without a destination alternate aerodrome, the aircraft must carry the fuel required to fly for 15 minutes at holding speed.
 
Since the holding fuel flow is 2 200 kg/hr or 2 200/60 kg/min, we have:
 
additional fuel = (15 minutes) x (2 200/60 kg/min) = (15x2200)/60 kg = 550 kg
 
Æ Note: In the old EASA AIR OPS regulations, the amount of fuel required to fly for 15 minutes at holding speed when a flight was operated without an alternate aerodrome was called additional fuel.
 
Final reserve fuel/energy:
According to EASA AIR OPS regulations, the final reserve fuel for a jet engine aircraft is fuel required to fly for 30 minutes at holding speed.
 
Since the holding fuel flow is 2 200 kg/hr or 2 200/60 kg/min, we have:
 
Final reserve fuel = (30 minutes) x (2 200/60 kg/min) = (30x2200)/60 kg = 1 100 kg
 
Extra fuel/energy:
This fuel/energy is required for anticipated delays or specific operational constraints. Since the question does not provide any information about the extra fuel, we can conclude that no extra fuel is carried for this flight. As a result, we have:
 
Extra fuel = 0 kg
 
Discretionary fuel/energy:
This fuel/energy is carried if required by the commander. Since the question does not provide any information about the discretionary fuel, we can conclude that no discretionary fuel is carried for this flight. As a result, we have:
 
Discretionary fuel = 0 kg
 
Based on the above, we can conclude that the total amount of fuel required for this commercial flight is:
 
Total fuel = 193.75 kg + 2 138.14 kg + 183.33 kg + 550 kg + 1 100 kg = 4 165.22 kg

question:
Refer to figure 033-70.
Using the simplified flight planning (alternate planning), find fuel required and trip time to alternate. Given:
 
Dry Operating Mass:	35 500 kg
Traffic Load:	14 500 kg
Final reserve fuel:	1 200 kg
Distance to alternate:	95 NM
Tailwind component:	10 kt

For pre-flight planning purposes, what is the total fuel required for the commercial air transport flight in a Medium Range Jet Transport (MRJT) aeroplane, with no destination alternate, given the following information?
 
Estimated taxi time:	20 minutes at both departure
and destination aerodromes
APU fuel flow:	115 kg/h
Taxi fuel flow:	11 kg/min
Planned APU usage
at the parking position:	zero
Total trip distance:	1 131 NM
Average fuel flow:	2 638 kg/h
True airspeed (TAS):	424 kt
Average wind component:	-17 kt
Contingency fuel:	3 % of the trip fuel
Holding fuel flow:	2 400 kg/h
Extra fuel:	0 kg
Discretionary fuel:	0 kg
explanation:
You need to realize the definition of the...

trip fuel => it is the fuel required for take-off from departure aerodrome, climb, cruise, descent, approach and landing at the destination aerodrome.

The other values provided by the question - contingency, alternate, final reserve and extra fuel are just reserves in case something does not go as planned. The trip fuel for this flight has been calculated as 4 300 kg and if the captain decides to take an additional extra fuel to increase his/her reserves the trip fuel still remains the same: 4 300 kg.

Note: On one hand - any increased trip fuel due to the higher mass cannot be calculated due to the lack of information provided by the question. When additional extra fuel is uplifted, the take-off mass increases and the original calculated trip fuel increases slightly as a result of this increased mass. However, on the other hand, the original trip fuel value is not changed on the Operational Flight Plan (OFP) - the slight fuel flow increase due to the increased mass is expected to be covered by the extra fuel. The surplus fuel burnoff penalty will always be a part of the total extra fuel, and have its own fuel flow rate, depending on the total extra fuel.

Example: For the Airbus A320-321, roughly 1 000 kg of extra fuel results in 20 kg of extra fuel consumption per hour, which is quite negligible (1%) considering the average 2 000 kg/hour fuel consumption. Anyway, the extra consumption caused by the extra fuel will be covered extra fuel added.


General information
EASA AIR OPS - Part-CAT AMC1 CAT.OP.MPA.150(b) Fuel policy - Planning Criteria - Aeroplanes
The operator should base the defined fuel policy, including calculation of the amount of fuel to be on board for departure, on the following planning criteria:

(a) Basic procedure
The usable fuel to be on board for departure should be the sum of the following:
(1) Taxi fuel, which should not be less than the amount expected to be used prior to take-off. Local conditions at the departure aerodrome and auxiliary power unit (APU) consumption should be taken into account.

(2) Trip fuel, which should include:
(i) fuel for take-off and climb from aerodrome elevation to initial cruising level/altitude, taking into account the expected departure routing;
(ii) fuel from top of climb to top of descent, including any step climb/descent;
(iii) fuel from top of descent to the point where the approach is initiated, taking into account the expected arrival procedure; and
(iv) fuel for approach and landing at the destination aerodrome.

(3) Contingency fuel, except as provided for in (b), which should be the higher of:
(i) Either:
(A) 5 % of the planned trip fuel or, in the event of in-flight replanning, 5 % of the trip fuel for the remainder of the flight;
(B) not less than 3 % of the planned trip fuel or, in the event of inflight replanning, 3 % of the trip fuel for the remainder of the flight, provided that an en-route alternate (ERA) aerodrome is available;
(C) an amount of fuel sufficient for 20 minutes flying time based upon the planned trip fuel consumption, provided that the operator has established a fuel consumption monitoring programme for individual aeroplanes and uses valid data determined by means of such a programme for fuel calculation; or
(D) an amount of fuel based on a statistical method that ensures an appropriate statistical coverage of the deviation from the planned to the actual trip fuel. This method is used to monitor the fuel consumption on each city pair/aeroplane combination and the operator uses this data for a statistical analysis to calculate contingency fuel for that city pair/aeroplane combination;
(ii) or an amount to fly for 5 minutes at holding speed at 1 500 ft (450 m), above the destination aerodrome in standard conditions.

(4) Alternate fuel, which should:
(i) include:
(A) fuel for a missed approach from the applicable DA/H or MDA/H at the destination aerodrome to missed approach altitude, taking into account the complete missed approach procedure;
(B) fuel for climb from missed approach altitude to cruising level/altitude, taking into account the expected departure routing;
(C) fuel for cruise from top of climb to top of descent, taking into account the expected routing;
(D) fuel for descent from top of descent to the point where the approach is initiated, taking into account the expected arrival procedure; and
(E) fuel for executing an approach and landing at the destination alternate aerodrome;
(ii) where two destination alternate aerodromes are required, be sufficient to proceed to the alternate aerodrome that requires the greater amount of alternate fuel.

(5) Final reserve fuel, which should be:
(i) for aeroplanes with reciprocating engines, fuel to fly for 45 minutes; or
(ii) for aeroplanes with turbine engines, fuel to fly for 30 minutes at holding speed at 1 500 ft (450 m) above aerodrome elevation in standard conditions, calculated with the estimated mass on arrival at the destination alternate aerodrome or the destination aerodrome, when no destination alternate aerodrome is required.

(6) The minimum additional fuel, which should permit:
(i) the aeroplane to descend as necessary and proceed to an adequate alternate aerodrome in the event of engine failure or loss of pressurisation, whichever requires the greater amount of fuel based on the assumption that such a failure occurs at the most critical point along the route, and
(A) hold there for 15 minutes at 1 500 ft (450 m) above aerodrome elevation in standard conditions; and
(B) make an approach and landing, except that additional fuel is only required if the minimum amount of fuel calculated in accordance with (a)(2) to (a)(5) is not sufficient for such an event; and
(ii) holding for 15 minutes at 1 500 ft (450 m) above destination aerodrome elevation in standard conditions, when a flight is operated without a destination alternate aerodrome.

(7) Extra fuel, which should be at the discretion of the commander.

explanation:
According to the Regulation (EU) 2 021/1296, specifically:
CAT.OP.MPA.181 Fuel/energy scheme – fuel/energy planning and in-flight re-planning policy – aeroplanes, and
CAT.OP.MPA.191 Fuel/energy scheme – Fuel/energy planning and in-flight re-planning policy – helicopters,
the operator shall ensure that the pre-flight calculation of the usable fuel that is required for a flight includes:
taxi fuel,
trip fuel,
contingency fuel, required to compensate for unforeseen factors that could have an influence on the fuel consumption to the destination aerodrome,
destination alternate fuel,
final reserve fuel,
additional fuel, if required by the type of operation (applicable ONLY to aeroplanes),
extra fuel to take into account anticipated delays or specific operational constraints, and
discretionary fuel, if required by the commander.
For this question, we assume no additional, extra or discretionary fuel since these type of fuels are carried if required by the operation or the commander. 

When landing at the destination alternate, the fuel values that normally will have been burnt are:
taxi fuel during taxi, 
trip fuel from the departure aerodrome to the destination aerodrome, and
destination alternate fuel from the destination aerodrome until the destination alternate.
Final reserve fuel must be maintained and not consumed during normal flight operations. Moreover, contingency fuel will or will not have been burnt depending on the existence of unforeseen factors that could have an influence on the fuel consumption to the destination aerodrome. Since the question practically asks about the absolute minimum amount of fuel in any case at the destination alternate, the correct answer is "final reserve fuel" because in the worst case scenario of unforeseen factors to the destination aerodrome, we are allowed to use the contingency fuel.

Æ Note: DISCRETIONARY fuel was implemented with EASAs Amendment 4 and has been called EXTRA fuel before. According to Amendment 4, extra fuel is now fuel for anticipated delays or specific operational constraints. EASA has not changed the calculations of the questions but only the wording. Since not all CAAs switch to the latest EASA ECQB at once, it is possible that some CAAs still show the older versions of the questions. Therefore we decided to keep old and new information within the questions and explanations.

The maximum possible discretionary fuel that can be loaded is equal to the lowest of the following discretionary fuel quantities:
Regulated Take-off Mass - Actual Take-off Mass = Regulated Take-off Mass - (Actual Zero Fuel Mass + Minimum Block Fuel - Taxi Fuel) = 76 000 kg - (59 031 kg + 8 000 kg - 240 kg) = 76 000 kg - 66 791 kg = 9 209 kg.
Maximum Landing Mass - Actual Landing Mass = Maximum Landing Mass - (Actual zero Fuel Mass + Minimum Block Fuel - Taxi Fuel - Trip Fuel) = 66 360 kg - (59 031 kg + 8 000 kg - 240 kg – 4 761 kg) = 66 360 kg - 62 030 kg = 4 330 kg.
Fuel Tank Capacity - Minimum Block Fuel = 16 000 kg – 8 000 kg = 8 000 kg.
From the above, we can conclude that the maximum possible discretionary fuel that can be loaded is: 4330 kg.
 
Æ Note: The Maximum Zero Fuel Mass need not to be considered for the calculation of the discretionary fuel.

options: A) Not able to carry extra fuel
correct_answer: A) Not able to carry extra fuel
explanation:
At the start of the take-off, the mass of the aircraft consists of Dry Operating Mass (DOM) + Traffic Load (TL) + Fuel (Trip + Contingency + Alternate + Final reserve). As the aircraft reaches destination it will have used the Trip fuel => therefore the fuel remaining in the tanks will be Contingency + Alternate + Final Reserve. From this information we can easily calculate the landing mass at destination in this way: DOM (33 000 kg) + TL (8 110 kg) + Alternate fuel (1 100 kg) + Contingency fuel (102 kg) + Final reserve fuel (983 kg) = 43 295 kg.

Note: There is often a lot of confusion about the contingency fuel - whether it is considered to have been used by the time the aircraft reached the destination or whether it remains in the tanks. If the flight goes as planned, the contingency fuel is not used and remains in the tanks upon reaching the destination. The purpose of the contingency fuel is to account for example for minor route alterations or basically if anything does not go exactly as planned (e.g. a deviation around a thunderstorm; stronger headwind than expected; lower cruising altitude than expected, etc...). Most of the questions ask about the planned masses - in this case you plan on not using the contingency fuel and the fuel on board when reaching the destination = Contingency + Alternate + Final reserve + any Extra fuel.

question: A flight is planned from Athens (Greece) to Paris (France) and back to Athens. Since it is not possible to refuel in Paris, the flight crew has to load enough fuel for both flights. Which of the following statements correctly describes the consequenses?

B) The fuel consumption will be less from Athens to Paris.

correct_answer: A) The CO2 emission will increase on the outbound leg from Athens to Paris.
explanation:
Trip fuel is given by: Average fuel flow x Trip time

Average fuel flow x (Route distance / Estimated GS) = 3 048 x [2 548 / (431 - 28)] = 19 271 kg

Contingency fuel: 3% x Trip fuel = 3% x 19 271 = 578 kg
Alternate fuel: Average fuel flow x Estimated flight time to the alternate = 3 048 x 34/60 = 1 727 kg
Final reserve: 1 351 kg

The take-off fuel is therefore: 19 271 + 578 + 1 727 + 1 351 = 22 927 kg, with the closest answer being 22 929 kg.

MORE INFO

AMC2 CAT.OP.MPA.150(b) Fuel policy   
LOCATION OF THE FUEL EN-ROUTE ALTERNATE (FUEL ERA) AERODROME
 
(a) The fuel ERA aerodrome should be located within a circle having a radius equal to 20 % of the total flight plan distance, the centre of which lies on the planned route at a distance from the destination aerodrome of 25 % of the total flight plan distance, or at least 20 % of the total flight plan distance plus 50 NM, whichever is greater. All distances should be calculated in still air conditions.

DISCRETIONARY fuel was implemented with EASAs Amendment 4 and has been called EXTRA fuel before. According to Amendment 4, extra fuel is now fuel for anticipated delays or specific operational constraints. EASA has not changed the calculations of the questions but only the wording. According to the figure 033-155, for a direct climb to the optimum altitude of 37 000 ft, we get a Brake Release Mass of 68 000 kg, which is more limiting than the Regulated Take-off Mass of 76 000 kg.
 
The maximum possible discretionary fuel will be equal to the lowest of the following discretionary Fuel quantities:
Brake Release Mass - Actual Take-off Mass = Brake Release Mass - (Actual Zero Fuel Mass + Minimum Block Fuel - Taxi Fuel)
= 68 000 kg - (59 031 kg + 8 000 kg - 240 kg)
= 68 000 kg - 66 791 kg = 1 209 kg.

Maximum Landing Mass - Actual Landing Mass = Maximum Landing Mass - (Actual zero Fuel Mass + Minimum Block Fuel - Taxi Fuel - Trip Fuel)
= 66 360 kg - (59 031 kg + 8 000 kg - 240 kg – 4 761 kg)
= 66 360 kg - 62 030 kg = 4 330 kg.

Fuel Tank Capacity - Minimum Block Fuel
= 16 000 kg – 8 000 kg = 8 000 kg.

From the above, we can conclude that the maximum possible discretionary duel  the aircraft can carry for a direct climb to 37 000 ft is 1 209 kg.
 
Æ Note 1: The Maximum Zero Fuel Mass need not to be considered for the calculation of the Discretionary Fuel.
 
Æ Note 2: Brake release mass is the mass of an aircraft at the start of a runway, just prior to brake release for take-off. This is the ramp mass minus any fuel used for taxi.

explanation:
Refer to figure 1238.
Caution! The question is easy but also tricky. Take your time and think twice before you answer such kind of questions.

Total fuel contains...
Taxi fuel
Trip fuel
Contingency fuel
Alternate fuel
Additional fuel
Final reserve fuel
Extra fuel
Discretionary fuel
As you can see - taxi, contingency, and any additional and reserve fuel are not part of the trip fuel.

Tip fuel contains fuel for...
take-off
climb
cruise (top of climb to top of descent + step climbs/descents)
descent (top of descent to initial approach fix)
approach
landing
Correct answer: take-off, climb, cruise including any step climb, descent, approach, and landing.

Æ Note: DISCRETIONARY fuel was implemented with EASAs Amendment 4 and has been called EXTRA fuel before. According to Amendment 4, extra fuel is now fuel for anticipated delays or specific operational constraints. EASA has not changed the calculations of the questions but only the wording. Since not all CAAs switch to the latest EASA ECQB at once, it is possible that some CAAs still show the older versions of the questions. Therefore we decided to keep old and new information within the questions and explanations.

Regulation for airplanes:
EASA AIR OPS
AMC1 CAT.OP.MPA.181 Fuel/energy scheme — fuel/energy planning and in-flight re-planning policy — aeroplanes
BASIC FUEL SCHEME — PRE-FLIGHT CALCULATION OF USABLE FUEL FOR PERFORMANCE CLASS A AEROPLANES
For the pre-flight calculation of the usable fuel in accordance with point CAT.OP.MPA.181, the operator should:
[...]
(b) for trip fuel, include:
(1) fuel for take-off and climb from the aerodrome elevation to the initial cruising level/altitude, taking into account the expected departure routing;
(2) fuel from the top of climb to the top of descent, including any step climb/descent;
(3) fuel from the top of descent to the point where the approach procedure is initiated, taking into account the expected arrival routing; and
(4) fuel for making an approach and landing at the destination aerodrome;
[...]

Regulation for helicopters:
EASA AIR OPS
AMC1 CAT.OP.MPA.191(b)&(c) Fuel/energy scheme — fuel/energy planning and in-flight re-planning policy —helicopters
PLANNING CRITERIA
(a) The pre-flight calculation of the required usable fuel to be carried on board should include the following:
[...]
(2) trip fuel, which should include fuel:
(i) for take-off and climb from the departure site elevation to the initial cruising level/altitude, taking into account the expected departure routing;
(ii) from the top of climb to the top of descent, including any step climb/descent;
(iii) from the top of descent to the point where the approach procedure is initiated, taking into account the expected arrival procedure; and
(iv) for the approach and landing at the destination site;
[...]

Given the following information for a commercial air transport operation, calculate the maximum possible extra fuel to be uplifted if the commander wants to climb directly to the optimum altitude of 37 000 ft.
 
Maximum Take-Off Mass (MTOM) to climb at FL370:	68 000 kg
Maximum Zero Fuel Mass (MZFM):	62 731 kg
Actual Zero Fuel Mass (AZFM):	59 031 kg
Regulated Take-Off Mass (RTOM):	76 000 kg
Maximum Landing Mass (MLM):	66 360 kg
Fuel Tank Capacity:	9 000 kg
Taxi Fuel:	240 kg
Trip Fuel:	4 761 kg
Minimum Block Fuel:	8 000 kg
explanation:
The maximum amount of discretionary fuel (old extra fuel) that can be carried is equal to the lowest of the following fuel quantities:
 
Maximum take-off mass (MTOM) - Actual take-off mass = MTOM - (Dry Operating mass + Traffic load + Planned take-off fuel) = 64 400 kg - (35 500kg + 14 500 kg + 7 400 kg) = 64 400 kg - 57 400 kg = 7 000 kg
 
Maximum landing mass (MLM) - Actual landing mass = MLM - (Actual take-off mass - trip fuel) = MLM - (Dry operating mass + Traffic load + Planned take-off fuel - Trip fuel) = 56 200 kg - (35 500kg + 14 500 kg + 7 400 kg - 4 900 kg) = 56 200 kg - 52 500 kg = 3 700 kg
 
Tank capacity - Block fuel = Tank capacity - (Planned take-off fuel + taxi fuel) / not given
 
We can see that the lowest calculated fuel quantity is 3 700 kg, i.e. the maximum amount of discretionary fuel is limited by the maximum landing mass (MLM).
 
Based on these calculations a discretionary amount of 3 700 kg can be loaded before departure.  However as the aircraft will now be heavier the Trip Fuel will be greater.  This additional Trip Fuel is calculated with the quoted Fuel Penalty as shown below.
 
Increased Trip Fuel = 75 x (3 700 / 1 000) = 278 kg.
 
Discretionary Fuel that can be loaded before departure = 3 700 + 278 = 3 978 kg, closest answer 4 000 kg.
 
This discretionary amount must not exceed the MTOM limitation or tank capacity if quoted, which in this case it doesn't.

So, discretionary fuel will be 4 000 kg ; and take-off fuel will be 11 400 kg (4 000 + 7 400)
 
Æ Note 1: Discretionary fuel was implemented in the latest EASA AIR OPS regulations (Regulation (EU) 2 021/1296) and it is the fuel which is added at the discretion of the commander - in the old EASA AIR OPS regulations, the fuel which was added at the discretion of the commander was called extra fuel. In the latest regulations, extra fuel is the fuel for anticipated delays or specific operational constraints. EASA has not changed the calculations of the questions but only the wording. Since not all CAAs switch to the latest EASA ECQB at once, it is possible that some CAAs still show the older versions of the questions. Therefore we decided to keep old and new information within the questions and explanations.

Æ Note 2: General information about the procedure:
We are sure that the following procedure is used by EASA because it was confirmed by a flight instructor who was in contact with EASA. Anyway, please let us know if you make different experiences in your official exams.

The maximum discretionary amount of fuel that can be carried is limited either by MTOM, MLM or Tank Capacity:

1) If MTOM is the limit (giving X kgs or lb of discretionary fuel) then as long as X + TOF does not exceed tank capacity, the answer is X.

2) If MLM is the limit (giving Y kgs or lb of discretionary fuel) then more than Y can be uplifted as discretionary fuel means a heavier aircraft which means more trip fuel.  This increase in trip fuel is described as a "fuel penalty (normally given as Z kgs or lb per 1 000 kgs or lb)" in the question.  This increase in trip fuel = (MLM discretionary / 1 000) x Z.  This increase in trip fuel will be burned en-route so arrival at the destination will be with the MLM discretionary intact.  The discretionary fuel that can be uploaded = MLM discretionary + increased trip fuel (MLM discretionary / 1 000) x Z.  This should be checked against MTOM discretionary and tank capacity.

3) The questions that state a fuel penalty and require an answer which is limited by MLM are currently excluded from the examinations by EASA and should not be asked as the solution process originally agreed upon has been changed - leading to confusion. If such question appears in your official exam, you can appeal (note from 2019-10-22: a student from Spain confirmed that such question appeared in his exam).

question: With two available destination alternates, specific distances, wind conditions, and fuel consumption graphs, how do you determine the minimum alternate fuel needed when the destination weather exceeds planning minima?
options: A) Use the alternate closest to the destination to calculate the minimum fuel.
B) Calculate for both alternates and use the higher minimum fuel value.
C) No alternate fuel is required as the weather is above planning minima.
D) Use the average of the required fuel for both alternates.
correct_answer: A) Use the alternate closest to the destination to calculate the minimum fuel.
explanation:
Refer to figure 2735.
The first step to solve the question is to determine the air distance an aircraft of 46 500 kg will cover per 1 000 kg of fuel burnt. For this purpose, we enter the table with 46 500 kg and with 45 500 kg and subtract the air miles found at 45 500 kg (2 072 NAM) from the 46 500 kg (2 257 NAM) value.
2 257 NAM – 2 072 NAM = 185 NAM

The TAS flown on these 185 NAM is the average from 46 500kg and 45 500 kg.
TAS at 46 500 kg = 414.5 kt (average between 46 000 kg and 47 000 kg)
TAS at 45 500 kg = 417.5 kt (average between 45 000 kg and 46 000 kg)

TAS = (414.5 kt + 417.5 kt/2) = 416 kt

At this TAS, the distance of 185 NAM will be covered within a flight time of
185 NAM : 416 NAM/h = 0.445 h

The next step is to determine the corresponding GROUND distances assuming the given tailwind components of 25 kt and 10 kt.
D(TWC25) = 0.445 h x (416 + 25) NM/h = 196.25 NM
D(TWC10) = 0.445 h x (416 + 10) NM/h = 189.57 NM

The mileage difference between tailwind components of 25 kt and 10 kt is therefore
189.57 NM – 196.25 NM = -6.7 NM ~ -7 NM

Or a decrease of 6.7 NM per 1 000 kg of fuel burnt.

Operating an MRJT aeroplance, you estimate to taxi for 10 minutes before take-off and operate the APU for 20 minutes before take-off. How much fuel will be used for these operations?
explanation:
The APU operates at a fuel consumption rate of 150 kg per hour. For 15 minutes of operation, the fuel consumption is::

150 kg/h : 60 min/h x 15 min = 37.5 kg.

Additionally, the APU startup requires 5 kg of fuel.

During taxi, the APU consumes fuel at a rate of 11 kg per minute. For a taxi duration of 10 minutes, the fuel consumption is:

11 kg/min x 10 min = 110 kg.

Thus, the total fuel consumption on the ground is:

37.5 kg + 5 kg + 110 kg = 152.5 kg or approximately 153 kg.

question:
Consider the following:

1) Before departure, the APU operates for 15 minutes.
2) The taxi time is expected to be 10 minutes.
3) The APU consumes an additional 5 kg of fuel during startup.
4) Due to modifications, the APU's fuel flow rate is 150 kg/hour, and the APU's fuel consumption during taxi is 11 kg/min.

Calculate the total APU fuel consumption on the ground.
explanation:
To calculate the required fuel to be on board before engine start, we need to consider several components:

taxi fuel,
trip fuel, 
contingency fuel,
alternate fuel, 
final reserve fuel,
extra fuel,
and discretionary fuel.

First, let's calculate the taxi fuel. This includes fuel for the APU and fuel for taxiing. The APU consumes 150 kg/h, but since it operates for 38 minutes, we convert this to kilograms per minute: 150 kg/h / 60 minutes/h = 2.5 kg/minute. For 38 minutes of APU operation, the fuel consumed is 2.5 kg/minute * 38 minutes = 95 kg. Additionally, taxiing consumes 11 kg/minute for 13 minutes, which equals 11 kg/minute * 13 minutes = 143 kg. Thus, the total taxi fuel is 95 kg (APU) + 143 kg (taxi) = 238 kg.

Next, we add the trip fuel (including contingency), which is given as 4 250 kg, and the alternate and final reserve fuel, given as 3 730 kg. Since there's no extra or discretionary fuel (both are 0 kg), these do not contribute to our total.

Therefore, the total required fuel on board before engine start is the sum of taxi fuel, trip fuel (including contingency), and alternate and final reserve fuel: 238 kg (taxi and start-up) + 4 250 kg (trip fuel) + 3 730 kg (alternate and final reserve) = 8 218 kg. However, considering rounding or minor calculation discrepancies based on the provided numbers, this closely aligns with the correct answer of 8 220 kg, suggesting a slight adjustment for rounding or precision in calculation.

explanation:
The question asks to calculate the total fuel for engine start and taxi.
 
From the question, we have: Fuel for engine start = 20 liters
 
Using the given fuel flow for taxi (150 l/hr) and the required time for taxiing (10 minutes), we will calculate the required taxi fuel with the help of the rule of three:
 
The aircraft burns 150 liters of fuel in 60 minutes (1 hour).
The aircraft burns x liters of fuel in 10 minutes.
 
We get: x = (10x150)/60 liters = 25 liters ⇒ taxi fuel = 25 liters
 
Since fuel for engine start = 20 liters and taxi fuel = 25 liters, we have:
 
Total fuel (Volume) = 20 liters and 25 liters = 45 liters
 
Given that the fuel density is 0.82 kg/l, the mass of this fuel quantity is:
 
Mass = Density x Volume = (0.82 kg/l) x 45 liters = 36.9 kg
 
Æ Note 1: The density, the mass and the volume of a substance are connected with the following relationship:
 
Density = Mass/Volume
 
Æ Note 2: If this question appears in your official exams, please provide us with any feedback that might help.

explanation:
EXPLANATION:
You are asked for the minimum usable fuel expected to remain on landing at the destination (London Heathrow). The applicable EASA regulation requires that the usable fuel expected to remain upon landing at the destination must not be less than the required alternate fuel plus the final reserve fuel when an alternate aerodrome is required. In this problem an alternate (East Midlands) is required, so the alternate fuel must be kept intact on arrival at the destination.

When the aircraft lands at the destination the taxi fuel and the trip fuel will normally have been burned. Contingency fuel may or may not have been burned depending on unforeseen factors. Because the question asks for the absolute minimum fuel that will be on board at destination in any case, we must assume the contingency fuel has been used (worst case). Final reserve and alternate fuel remain intact. Therefore the minimum expected fuel on arrival = Alternate fuel + Final reserve fuel.
Compute: 1 200 kg + 1 300 kg = 2 500 kg

APPLICABLE REGULATION:
EASA AIR OPS
ED Decision 2022/005/R
AMC1 CAT.OP.MPA.185(a) Fuel/energy scheme — in-flight fuel/energy management policy — aeroplanes
[...]
(b) In-flight fuel management
(1) The flight should be conducted to ensure that the usable fuel expected to remain upon landing at the destination aerodrome is not less than:
(i) the required alternate fuel plus the FRF; or
(ii) the FRF if no alternate aerodrome is required.
[...]
 
EASA AIR OPS
ED Decision 2022/005/R
AMC1 CAT.OP.MPA.195 Fuel/energy scheme — in-flight fuel/energy management policy — helicopters
ENSURING A SAFE LANDING FOR COMPLEX MOTOR-POWERED HELICOPTERS IN OTHER THAN LOCAL OPERATIONS
The operator should base in-flight fuel management procedures on the following criteria:
[...]
(b) in-flight fuel management:
(1) if an in-flight fuel check shows that the usable fuel that is expected to remain upon landing at the destination is less than the required alternate fuel plus the FRF, the commander should:
(i) divert; or
(ii) replan the flight in accordance with point SPA.HOFO.120(b)(1) unless the commander considers it safer to proceed to the destination; and
[…]

explanation:
Discretionary fuel is carried at the commander's sole discretion and is not subject to influence from operators or other parties. It exists precisely for situations like unplanned events that increase fuel requirements beyond what was originally calculated.

In this scenario, you have 30 minutes of discretionary fuel and face a 15-minute increase in taxi time. The commander may elect to use the discretionary fuel to cover this additional taxi requirement. Since the delay (15 minutes) is less than the discretionary fuel available (30 minutes), departure can proceed without uplifting additional fuel.

question: You’re at the gate, prepared for departure, with 30 minutes of discretionary fuel onboard. ATC then advises that due to a runway closure, taxi time will be increased by 15 minutes. What is the appropriate course of action in this situation?
options: A) You may proceed without any further fuel consideration, regardless of the taxi delay.
B) You can use discretionary fuel for the taxi and depart.
C) You cannot depart because the contingency fuel is not enough to compensate for the taxi delay.
D) You cannot depart and need to add more taxi fuel.
correct_answer: B) You can use discretionary fuel for the taxi and depart.

question:
When planning a commercial flight with no alternate, the trip fuel includes:

Refer to figures 033-37, 033-38, 033-107 and 033-106.
Given:
 
Mass at brake release:	210 000 kg
Selected cruise speed:	Mach 0.82
Flight leg distance:	3 000 NM
Cruise level:	OPTIMUM
Air conditioning:	  STANDARD
Anti-icing:	OFF
Temperature:	ISA
CG:	37 %

Assuming zero wind, the planned Landing Mass at the destination will be:
explanation:
Refer to figure 456.
1. Step: INTEGRATED CRUISE table
General: please disregard that the question uses the term "mass" and the attached documents use the term "weight". We are sorry that we cannot tell you why EASA does not use the same units. You will find the values for the distances and times by using the intersections which will result from the corresponding values on the first left column and the first row on the top. Example: if you want to know the distance for 194 800 kg, you have to enter the table on the left side, at "194", then continue horizontally to the right until you intersect the column ".8" (.8 = 800 kg). The result will be 6 812 NM. You can also start at the ".8" column first, then vertically downwards until you intersect the "194" row - it's up to you.

Back to the question: the mass at brake release is 210 000 kg, therefore we have to follow the first column downwards to find the value of "210", then horizontally to the right until you intersect the "0" column, which corresponds to the first box. The distance given is 8 019 NM. We do not have to consider any wind because the question states that the wind is zero. Now subtract the planned distance of 3 000 NM from this value: 8 019 NM - 3 000 NM = 5 019 NM. Search this distance in the table (upper left corner) and read the corresponding value of 174 200 kg.

2. Step: CLIMB CORRECTION table
The result of 174 200 kg has to be corrected by using the climb correction table. The question states that the aircraft will fly at the optimum flight level, therefore we have to use the table at the bottom. On the first column on the left you will find our cruising speed of M.82, continue horizontally to the right until you intersect the "210" column to find a result of 2.9 (= 2 900 kg). The fuel consumption is higher during the climb, therefore we have to subtract this value from 174 200 kg (due to the increased fuel consumption, the aircraft will be lighter): 174 200 kg - 2 900 kg = 171 300 kg.

3. Step: DESCENT CORRECTION table
We haven't finished the calculations yet because we also have to consider the descent correction. Please use the last document and enter the table at the bottom. Note that this table refers to the optimum flight level. A value of 170 gives a result of 1.0 (= 1 000 kg). Our result from above is 171 300 kg but the table also gives a result of 1.0 for a value of 180 000 kg, therefore we do not have to do any interpolation. During a descent the thrust levers are pulled back and the aircraft consumes less fuel, therefore we have to add the correction to our result from above: 171 300 kg + 1 000 kg = 172 300 kg.

Given:

The ATC instructs the pilot of an aircraft to adjust the route during the cruise phase in order to avoid an airspace ahead due to military activities, increasing the distance to be flown. The amount of fuel required as a result of the increased flight distance is included in:

explanation:
Under the Basic Fuel Scheme WITHOUT variations (AMC1 CAT.OP.MPA.181), block fuel consists of:
taxi fuel + trip fuel + contingency fuel + alternate fuel + final reserve fuel

1. Trip fuel:
Departure and climb: 30 l
Cruise (1h 50m): 110/60 × 58 = 106.3 l
Approach and landing: 14 l
Trip fuel = 150.3 l

2. Contingency fuel (higher of):
5% of trip fuel: 0.05 × 150.3 = 7.5 l
5 min holding: 5/60 × 50 = 4.2 l
Contingency = 7.5 l

3. Alternate fuel:
Go-around and climb: 30 l
Cruise back: 106.3 l
Approach and landing: 14 l
Alternate fuel = 150.3 l

4. Final reserve fuel (Final reserve fuel (reciprocating-engine aeroplane under AMC1 CAT.OP.MPA.181 / CAT.OP.MPA.151): 45 minutes at holding consumption.):
FRF = 45/60 × 50 = 37.5 l

5. Taxi fuel: 8 l

Block fuel = 8 + 150.3 + 7.5 + 150.3 + 37.5 = 353.6 ≈ 354 l

correct_answer: D) (1) unforeseen or unpredictable factors; (2) destination

question: Delays are expected at the destination airfield, so the flight crew decides to include an extra 15 minutes of endurance fuel. Such fuel is part of the...
explanation:
Extra fuel is carried for situations that are known and anticipated before departure, not for unforeseen circumstances.

Expected higher fuel consumption due to a known MEL item is the only scenario that isn't an unforeseen circumstance.

The other three options all describe unforeseen events:

Unforeseen weather phenomena
Unanticipated runway closure
Unexpected air traffic delays

question: Extra fuel is carried for situations that may require more fuel than planned. Which scenario justifies adding extra fuel?
options: A) Unexpected air traffic delays at the alternate airport
B) Expected higher fuel consumption due to a known MEL item
C) Unforeseen weather phenomena en route
D) Unanticipated runway closure at the destination
correct_answer: B) Expected higher fuel consumption due to a known MEL item

explanation:
The correct method for determining additional fuel needed to reach an alternate airport is to calculate based on the expected headwinds or tailwinds.

When planning fuel requirements for an alternate airport, you must consider the actual conditions you're likely to encounter during that segment of flight. Wind has a significant impact on ground speed and, therefore, on flight time and fuel consumption.

Since the aircraft has a fuel consumption rate of 5 000 kg/h, the time spent flying to the alternate directly affects fuel requirements. A headwind would increase flight time and thus increase fuel consumption, while a tailwind would reduce both.

Excluding the remaining options:

Climb option: is irrelevant since it only mentions the climb, leaving out the rest of the flight.
Standard reserve options: refers to final reserve fuel, not alternate fuel.
Contingency option: contingency is a separate fuel category and is not specifically for calculating alternate fuel.

question: What method should be used to determine the additional fuel needed to reach an alternate airport at 2 000 ft, given an aircraft fuel consumption rate of 5 000 kg/h?
options: B) Calculate based on the extra time needed to climb to 2 000 ft
C) Calculate using expected headwinds or tailwinds
D) Calculate based on standard reserve requirements
correct_answer: C) Calculate using expected headwinds or tailwinds

explanation:
Start by finding each time interval:
Second taxi = s
First taxi = 2s
Engine shutdown (APU on) = 2s  (equal to first taxi)

2s + 2s + s = 60 min (=) s = 12 min

First taxi to remote stand: 24 min
Engine shutdown (APU running): 24 min
Second taxi to runway: 12 min

Fuel consumed by engine running:
Engine taxi time = 24 + 12 = 36 min = 0.6 h
Taxi fuel = 900 × 0.6 = 540 kg

Fuel consumed by APU:
APU time = 24 min = 0.4 h
APU fuel = 150 × 0.4 = 60 kg

Total taxi fuel = 540 + 60 = 600 kg

The first taxi segment lasted twice as long as the second taxi segment and the engine shutdown at the remote stand lasted as long as the first taxi segment.

explanation:
Refer to figure 2739.
First we need to find the fuel flow - in the first line of the table, find a section for 45% power (last column) => directly underneath read the fuel flow of 16 GPH.

The total flight time will be 107 minutes (1 hr 47 min) + 13 minutes for climb and descent (= 120 min), which comes out to a total of 2 hrs.

The Trip Fuel is therefore 32.0 gallons (2 × 16).

Fuel reserve is 30% of Trip Fuel => 32× 0.3 => approx. 9.6 gallons.

Block Fuel is therefore 32 + 9.6 + 5 (startup and taxi) => 46.6 gallons.

Note: No temperature corrections are necessary for the Manifold Pressure (MAP) since the ISA temp at FL100 is -5°C.

Given:

From the data given below, calculate the maximum amount of discretionary fuel (formerly called extra fuel).
 
MZFM:	2 000 kg
AZFM:	2 000 kg
RTOM:	2 900 kg
MLM:	2 600 kg
Trip fuel:	350 kg
Minimum block fuel:	500 kg
Fuel tank capacity:	950 kg
explanation:
AMC1 CAT.OP.MPA.181 Fuel/energy scheme — fuel/energy planning and in-flight re-planning policy — aeroplanes
BASIC FUEL SCHEME — PRE-FLIGHT CALCULATION OF USABLE FUEL FOR PERFORMANCE CLASS A AEROPLANES
For the pre-flight calculation of the usable fuel in accordance with point CAT.OP.MPA.181, the operator should:
[…]
(c) for contingency fuel, calculate for unforeseen factors either:
(1) 5 % of the planned trip fuel or, in the event of in-flight re-planning, 5 % of the trip fuel for the remainder of the flight; or
(2) an amount to fly for 5 minutes at holding speed at 1 500 ft (450 m) above the destination aerodrome in standard conditions, 
whichever is the higher;
[…]


The contingency fuel in the Basic Fuel Scheme is the higher of 5% of planned trip fuel or 5 minutes at holding speed at 1 500 ft above destination in standard conditions. Here the calculation is pre-flight, so we use the full planned Trip Fuel.

First, compute 5% of Trip Fuel.
0.05 × 3 420 kg = 171 kg

Second, compute 5 minutes at holding speed using the given holding fuel flow of 2 376 kg/h.
5 min = 5/60 h = 1/12 h
2 376 kg/h × 1/12 h = 2 376/12 kg = 198 kg

Now select the higher value in accordance with AMC1 CAT.OP.MPA.181(c).
max(171 kg, 198 kg) = 198 kg

Therefore, the Contingency Fuel is 198 kg.
