explanation:
Taxi: 5 l
Trip: 83 l
Alternate: 18 l
Contingency:  5 % of trip =  83 l × 0.05 = 4.15 l (check 5 minutes: 35 l / 60 min x 5 min = 3 litre = less than 5 %)
Final reserve: 35 l × 0.75 = 26.25 l
Total: 5 l + 83 l + 18 l + 4.15 l + 26.25 l =  136.4 l (136 l = closest answer)

Note: Students confirmed that 136 l was provided in their exams, so we have to round down, even if it would be safer to round up. Information about additional fuel is not provided by the question. If we would add 15 min additional fuel (8.75 l), the total fuel would be 145.15 l, which is not provided by any answer. Students reported all answers for this question so the highest is 141 l and it is very likely that EASA wants 136 l.

EASA AIR OPS
Part-CAT CAT AMC2 CAT.OP.MPA.181 Fuel/energy scheme — fuel/energy planning and in-flight re-planning policy — aeroplanes
BASIC FUEL SCHEME — PRE-FLIGHT CALCULATION OF USABLE FUEL FOR PERFORMANCE CLASS B and C AEROPLANES
The pre-flight calculation of required usable fuel should include:
taxi fuel, if significant;
trip fuel;
contingency fuel that is not less than 5 % of the planned trip fuel, or in the event of in-flight re-planning, 5 % of the trip fuel for the remainder of the flight;
alternate fuel to reach the destination alternate aerodrome via the destination if a destination alternate aerodrome is required;
FRF to comply with point CAT.OP.MPA.181(c);
extra fuel if there are anticipated delays or specific operational constraints; and
discretionary fuel, if required by the commander.
The operating conditions may include rounded-up figures of fuel for all flights.

CAT.OP.MPA.181 Fuel/energy scheme – fuel/energy planning and in-flight re-planning policy – aeroplanes

(5) final reserve fuel/energy that shall be the amount of fuel/energy that is calculated at holding speed at 1 500ft (450 m) above the aerodrome elevation in standard conditions according to the aeroplane estimated mass on arrival at the destination alternate aerodrome, or destination aerodrome when no destination alternate aerodrome is required, and shall not be less than:
for aeroplanes with reciprocating engines, the fuel/energy to fly for 45 minutes; or
(for turbine-engined aeroplanes, the fuel/energy to fly for 30 minutes;

Æ Note: DISCRETIONARY fuel was implemented with EASAs Amendment 4 and has been called EXTRA fuel before. According to Amendment 4, extra fuel is now fuel for anticipated delays or specific operational constraints. EASA has not changed the calculations of the questions but only the wording. Since not all CAAs switch to the latest EASA ECQB at once, it is possible that some CAAs still show the older versions of the questions. Therefore we decided to keep old and new information within the questions and explanations.

question:
Refer to figure 033-70.
What is the fuel and time to alternate? Given:
 
Estimated dry operation mass:	35 500 kg
Estimated load:	14 500 kg
Final reserve fuel:	1 200 kg
Distance to alternate:	95 NM
Average true track:	219°
Headwind component:	10 kt

Given:
 
Maximum Take-off Weight (MTOW):	4 290 kg
Maximum Landing Weight (MLW):	3 860 kg
Zero Fuel Weight (ZFW):	3 230 kg
Take-off fuel:	910 kg
Trip fuel:	730 kg

The captain wants to load 120 kg of discretionary fuel (formerly called extra fuel). Is this possible?
options: C) Yes, the captain can, but, must burn 330 kg of fuel at destination before landing.
D) Yes, the captain can, but must burn 30 kg of fuel at destination before landing.
explanation:
EASA AIR OPS
Regulation (EU) 2021/1296
CAT.OP.MPA.181 Fuel/energy scheme – fuel/energy planning and in-flight re-planning policy – aeroplanes
[...]
(c) The operator shall ensure that the pre-flight calculation of the usable fuel/energy that is required for a flight includes:
(1) taxi fuel/energy [...]
(2) trip fuel/energy [...]
(3) contingency fuel/energy that shall be the amount of fuel/energy required to compensate for unforeseen factors;
(4) destination alternate fuel/energy [...]
(5) final reserve fuel/energy [...]
(6) additional fuel/energy, if required by the type of operation; [...]
(7) extra fuel/energy to take into account anticipated delays or specific operational constraints; and
(8) discretionary fuel/energy, if required by the commander.
[...]

The question practically asks to calculate the MINIMUM block fuel according to the above regulation. From the values given, tankering fuel is not included in the MINIMUM required fuel and it's a separate item. Tankering fuel is the practice of carrying more fuel than required for the outbound flight so it can be used on the return or next sector, usually to save costs when fuel at the destination is more expensive or less available. If it was included in the minimum block fuel, it would be categorized as either extra or discretionary fuel. Therefore, to calculate the minimum block fuel we need to sum all the given values, except the tankering fuel:

Minimum block fuel = Taxi fuel + Trip fuel + Contingency fuel + Destination alternate fuel + Final reserve fuel
Minimum block fuel = 100 lb + 2 862 lb + 143 lb + 494 lb + 430 lb
Minimum block fuel = 4 029 lb

explanation:
Refer to figure 2051.
The table contained in the figure 033-125 is based on ISA conditions and the distance values depicted are in nautical air miles (air distance).
 
The distance provided by the question (2 000 NM) is ground distance. Thus, if we have ISA conditions and calm wind, then GS = TAS and therefore we have: Air distance = 2 000 NAM
 
From the table we can see that:
A gross mass of 43 500 kg gives a distance of: 1 995 NAM
A gross mass of 43 600 kg gives a distance of: 2 017 NAM
 
With interpolation we get that the gross mass that gives a distance of 2 000 NAM is: 43 523 kg
 
As a result, the fuel required to cover a distance of 2 000 NAM is: 43 523 kg - 35 000 kg = 8 523 kg
 
We will now examine each change separately:
 
Headwind component changes from 0 kt to 30 kt plus a change in OAT from ISA to ISA +5 °C:
According to note 2 below the table, if the outside air temperature (OAT) increases by 5°C, from ISA+0°C to ISA+5°C, the following adjustments have to be made:
 
a) The TAS will increase by 5 kt and will become: TAS = 424 kt + 5 kt = 429 kt
 
b) The required fuel consumption must increase by: 0.3%
 
If the headwind component is 30 kt, the ground speed (GS) will be: GS = 429 kt - 30 kt = 399 kt
 
As a result, the air distance that corresponds to a ground distance of 2 000 NM is:
2 000 x (429/399) NAM = 2 150.37 NAM ≈ 2 150 NAM
 
From the table we can see that:
A gross mass of 44 200 kg gives a distance of: 2 148 NAM
A gross mass of 44 300 kg gives a distance of: 2 170 NAM
 
With interpolation we can get that the gross mass that gives a distance of 2 150 NAM is: 44 209 kg
 
As a result, the fuel required to cover a distance of 2 150 NAM is: 44 209 kg - 35 000 kg = 9 209 kg
 
Since the fuel required must increase by 0.3%, the final fuel required to cover the distance of 2 150 NAM is:
 
9 209 kg + 0.003 x 9 209 kg = 9 237 kg
 
This means that the fuel required will increase by: ((9237-8 523)/8523) x 100 = 8.38%
 
Headwind component changes from 0 kt to 30 kt plus a change in OAT from ISA to ISA -10 °C:
According to note 2 below the table, if the outside air temperature (OAT) decreases by 10°C, from ISA+0°C to ISA -10°C, the following adjustments have to be made:
 
a) The TAS will decrease by 10 kt and will become: TAS = 424 kt - 10 kt = 414 kt
 
b) The required fuel consumption will decrease by: 0.6%
 
If the headwind component is 30 kt, the ground speed (GS) will be: GS = 414 kt - 30 kt = 384 kt
 
As a result, the air distance that corresponds to a ground distance of 2 000 NM is: 
2 000 x (414/384) NAM = 2 156.25 NAM ≈ 2 156 NAM
 
From the table we can see that:
A gross mass of 44 200 kg gives a distance of: 2 148 NAM
A gross mass of 44 300 kg gives a distance of: 2 170 NAM
 
With interpolation we can get that the gross mass that gives a distance of 2 156 NAM is: 44 236 kg
 
As a result, the fuel required to cover a distance of 2 156 NAM is: 44 236 kg - 35 000 kg = 9 236 kg
 
Since the fuel required must decrease by 0.6%, the final fuel required to cover the distance of 2 156 NAM is:
 
9 236 kg - 0.006 x 9 236 kg = 9 181 kg
 
This means that the fuel required will increase by: ((9181-8 523)/8523) x 100 = 7.72%
 
OAT changes from ISA to ISA +20 °C:
According to note 2 below the table, if the outside air temperature (OAT) increases by 20°C, from ISA+0°C to ISA+20°C, the following adjustment has to be made:
 
The required fuel consumption will increase by: 1.2%
 
Headwind component changes from 0 kt to 50 kt:
If the outside air temperature (OAT) is ISA, then the TAS will be: 424 kt
 
If the headwind component is 50 kt, the ground speed (GS) will be: GS = 424 kt - 50 kt = 374 kt
 
As a result, the air distance that corresponds to a ground distance of 2 000 NM is: 
2 000 x (424/374) NAM = 2 267.38 NAM ≈ 2 267 NAM
 
From the table we can see that:
A gross mass of 44 700 kg gives a distance of: 2 257 NAM
A gross mass of 44 800 kg gives a distance of: 2 279 NAM
 
With interpolation we can get that the gross mass that gives a distance of 2 267 NAM is: 44 745 kg
 
As a result, the fuel required to cover a distance of 2 267 NAM is: 44 745 kg - 35 000 kg = 9 745 kg
 
This means that the fuel required will increase by: ((9745-8 523)/8523) x 100 = 14.34%
 
Based on the above, we can conclude that the greatest increase in trip fuel required will be encountered by the following change:
 
Headwind component changes from 0 kt to 50 kt
 
Æ Note 1: As the aircraft mass is not given, you do not necessarily have to enter the table at 2 000 nm (or 2 150 nm for the headwind adjustment). You can choose any two mileages that have a difference of 2 000 nm respectively, which would also lead to different TAS. However, in principle, the changes should have the same trend, the exact numbers might be different, but e.g. a temperature change should yield a smaller change to the fuel than headwind change.
 
Æ Note 2: In the explanation we used the following relationship which connects the air distance with the ground distance:
 
Air distance/True airspeed = Ground distance/Ground speed   or
 
Air distance = Ground distance (True airspeed/Ground speed)
 
Æ Note 3: Example of interpolation:
We want to calculate the gross mass that gives an air distance of 2 156 NAM.
 
From the table we can see that:
A gross mass of 44 200 kg gives a distance of: 2 148 NAM
A gross mass of 44 300 kg gives a distance of: 2 170 NAM
 
As the gross mass increases by 100 kg (44 200→44 300), the air distance increases by 22 NAM (2 148→2 170).
As the gross mass increases by x kg, the air distance increases by 8 NAM (2 148 →2 156).
 
We get: x = (8x100)/22 kg = 36.36 kg ≈ 36 kg ⇒ the gross mass that gives an air distance of 2 156 NAM is:
 
44 200 kg + 36 kg = 44 236 kg
 
Æ Note 4: If this question appears in your official exams, please provide us with any feedback that might help.

question:
Refer to figure 033-125.
Given:
 
Cruise level:	FL370
Cruise speed:	M 0.74
Trip distance:	2 000 NM

The greatest increase in trip fuel required will be encountered by the following change(s):
options: A) Headwind component changes from 0 kt to 50 kt.
B) Headwind component changes from 0 kt to 30 kt plus a change in OAT from ISA to ISA +5 °C.
C) Headwind component changes from 0 kt to 30 kt plus a change in OAT from ISA to ISA -10 °C.
D) OAT changes from ISA to ISA +20 °C.
correct_answer: A) Headwind component changes from 0 kt to 50 kt.
explanation:
Let's start by establishing the ISA temperature at FL350.
ISA temperature = 15°C − (2°C × 35) = 15°C − 70°C = −55°C

The initial OAT is −55°C. So, OAT = ISA (ISA deviation = 0).

Read the initial NAM and TAS from the figure (033-139):
At 50 000 kg and ISA conditions:
- Air range (NAM) = 3 261 NAM
- TAS = 426 kt

Apply the two changes:
Change 1 – Mass decreases by 1 000 kg (50 000 → 49 000 kg):
This means that the air range changes from 3 261 NAM to 3 063 NAM.

Change 2 – OAT changes to −45°C:
ISA deviation = −45°C − (−55°C) = +10°C above ISA
Per NOTE 2 of figure 033-139: for each +1°C above ISA, TAS increases by 1 kt. So, TAS increases by 10 kt: new TAS = 426 + 10 = 436 kt


Convert NAM to NGM using the formula: NGM = NAM × (GS / TAS)

The headwind component is 25 kt (given in the question), unchanged in both scenarios.

So, we have:
Scenario 1:
- TAS = 426 kt, GS = 426 − 25 = 401 kt, NAM = 3 261
- NGM₁ = 3 261 × (401 / 426) = 3 261 × 0.9413 = 3 070 NGM

Scenario 2:
- TAS = 436 kt, GS = 436 − 25 = 411 kt, NAM = 3 063
- NGM₂ = 3 063 × (411 / 436) = 3 063 × 0.9427 = 2 887 NGM

The difference will be 3 070 − 2 887 = 183 NGM

Given:

Contingency fuel:	included in trip fuel
Final reserve fuel:	1 250 kg
Estimated APU usage:	38 min
Estimated taxi time:	13 min
APU fuel flow:	115 kg/h
Taxi fuel flow:	11 kg/min

The minimum usable fuel required for the commercial flight is:
explanation:
The maximum amount of discretionary fuel (old extra fuel) that can be carried is equal to the lowest of the following fuel quantities:
 
Maximum take-off mass (MTOM) - Actual take-off mass = MTOM - (Dry Operating mass + Traffic load + Planned take-off fuel) = 233 000 kg - (122 550 kg + 35 367 kg + 67 885 kg) = 233 000 kg - 225 802 kg = 7 198 kg
 
Maximum landing mass (MLM) - Actual landing mass = MLM - (Actual take-off mass - trip fuel) = MLM - (Dry operating mass + Traffic load + Planned take-off fuel - Trip fuel) = 182 000 kg - (122 550 kg + 35 367 kg + 67 885 kg - 62 332 kg) = 182 000 kg - 163 470 kg = 18 530 kg
 
Tank capacity - Block fuel = Tank capacity - (Planned take-off fuel + taxi fuel) = 110 000 kg - (67 885 kg + 300 kg) = 110 000 kg - 68 185 kg = 41 815 kg
 
We can see that the lowest calculated fuel quantity is 7 198 kg, i.e. the maximum amount of discretionary fuel is limited by the maximum take-off mass (MTOM).
 
Thus, it is not required to apply any fuel penalty and therefore, the total amount of discretionary fuel that can be carried is: 7 198 kg
 
Æ Note 1: Discretionary fuel was implemented in the latest EASA AIR OPS regulations (Regulation (EU) 2 021/1296) and it is the fuel which is added at the discretion of the commander - in the old EASA AIR OPS regulations, the fuel which was added at the discretion of the commander was called extra fuel. In the latest regulations, extra fuel is the fuel for anticipated delays or specific operational constraints. EASA has not changed the calculations of the questions but only the wording. Since not all CAAs switch to the latest EASA ECQB at once, it is possible that some CAAs still show the older versions of the questions. Therefore we decided to keep old and new information within the questions and explanations.

Æ Note 2: General information about the procedure:
We are sure that the following procedure is used by EASA because it was confirmed by a flight instructor who was in contact with EASA. Anyway, please let us know if you make different experiences in your official exams.

The maximum discretionary amount of fuel that can be carried is limited either by MTOM, MLM or Tank Capacity:

1) If MTOM is the limit (giving X kgs or lb of discretionary fuel) then as long as X + TOF does not exceed tank capacity, the answer is X.

2) If MLM is the limit (giving Y kgs or lb of discretionary fuel) then more than Y can be uplifted as discretionary fuel means a heavier aircraft which means more trip fuel.  This increase in trip fuel is described as a "fuel penalty (normally given as Z kgs or lb per 1 000 kgs or lb)" in the question.  This increase in trip fuel = (MLM discretionary / 1 000) x Z.  This increase in trip fuel will be burned en-route so arrival at the destination will be with the MLM discretionary intact.  The discretionary fuel that can be uploaded = MLM discretionary + increased trip fuel (MLM discretionary / 1 000) x Z.  This should be checked against MTOM discretionary and tank capacity.

3) The questions that state a fuel penalty and require an answer which is limited by MLM are currently excluded from the examinations by EASA and should not be asked as the solution process originally agreed upon has been changed - leading to confusion. If such question appears in your official exam, you can appeal (note from 2019-10-22: a student from Spain confirmed that such question appeared in his exam).

question:
Given:

Load:	7 600 kg

explanation:
Refer to figure 1713.
Please refer to the figure 033-164 which gives information about the company’s fuel policy.
 
According to the company’s fuel policy, the taxi fuel comprises the fuel for start up and taxiing, including the APU consumption.
 
Additionally, for the calculation of the taxi fuel, the following information is provided:
The average fuel quantity for start up, which is 5 kg.
Fuel consumption during taxiing is 5 kg/min.
APU consumption is 120 kg/h.
The expected taxi time is 10 minutes. Given that the fuel consumption during taxiing is 5 kg/min, the expected fuel consumption during taxiing is:

(10 x 5)/1 kg = 50 kg

Moreover, the APU will be ON for 5 minutes. Given that the APU consumption is 120 kg/h (120 kg/h = 120/60 kg/min = 2 kg/min), the expected fuel consumption during the operation of the APU is:
 
(2 x 5)/1 kg = 10 kg
 
Eventually, the total taxi fuel for this flight is:
 
5 kg + 50 kg + 10 kg = 65 kg

Given:

Forecast wind component:	-15 kt

options: A) 32 kg less than originally planned.
B) 39 kg less than originally planned.
C) 60 kg less than originally planned.
D) 28 kg less than originally planned.
correct_answer: A) 32 kg less than originally planned.
explanation:
Refer to figure 1979.
In the PLANNED FUEL section of the attached operation flight plan we can see that the mass of the initial trip fuel, i.e. of the trip fuel without the additional cargo of 600 kg, is 14 636 kg.
 
Please refer now to the OPERATIONAL IMPACTS section.
 
In the second line of this section, we can see the following information:
 
When the weight increases (UP) by 1 000 kg (1.0), the trip fuel must increase (UP) by 140 kg.
 
Based on this information and using the rule of three we can calculate the additional trip fuel required for an additional cargo with a mass of 600 kg:
 
When the weight increases by 1 000 kg the trip fuel increases by 140 kg.
When the weight increases by 600 kg the trip fuel increases by x kg.
 
We get: x = (600x140)/1000 kg = 84 kg
 
In the fourth line of the same section, we can also see the following information:
 
When the cruise level decreases (DN) by one flight level (FL1 or 1 000 ft), the trip fuel must increase (P) by 206 kg.
 
Based on this information and using the rule of three we can calculate the additional trip fuel required for flying 2 000 ft lower the initially planned cruise altitude:
 
When the cruise level changes by 1 000 ft the trip fuel increases by 206 kg.
When the cruise level changes by 2 000 ft the trip fuel increases by y kg.
 
We get: y = (206x2000)/1000 kg = 412 kg
 
Eventually, the new trip fuel will be:
 
Trip fuel (new) = 14 636 kg + 84 kg + 412 kg = 15 132 kg

question:
Refer to figure 033-178.
In the attached figure you can see a typical airline fuel plan with operational impact factors produced by a flight planning tool. Arriving at the aircraft, the crew is informed that additional cargo with a mass of 600 kg has been loaded and must be taken to the destination in any case. As a result, the aircraft is not able to fly at the planned cruising level but at a level which is 2 000 ft lower.

If the planned cruising speed remains unchanged, the new trip fuel will be…

Note: disregard time correction

A jet aeroplane has a cruising fuel consumption of 4 060 kg/hr, and 3 690 kg/hr during holding. If the destination is an isolated airfield, the aeroplane must carry, in addition to contingency reserves, additional fuel of:
explanation:
Refer to figures 1238 and 1478.
The minimum take off fuel required is found from the greatest of the two sums: sum(d)(1) and sum(d)(2), where sum(d)(1) and sum(d)(2) are the sums of the usable fuel on board for departure which are calculated separately according to Part-CAT AMC6 CAT.OP.MPA.181 Fuel/energy scheme — fuel/energy planning and in-flight re-planning policy — aeroplanes (page 3 of attachment 1 238)

We have for each sum:
 
sum (d)(1)
Trip fuel from departure aerodrome to destination 1 aerodrome, via the decision point = Flight time from the departure aerodrome to the destination 1 aerodrome, via the decision point x Average fuel flow = (375/60 hr) x (3 010 kg/hr) = (375x3010)/60 kg = 18 812.5 kg
 
Contingency fuel = 5% of the fuel required to fly from the decision point to destination 1 aerodrome = 0.05 x Flight time from the decision point to the destination 1 aerodrome x Average fuel flow = 0.05 x (106/60 hr) x (3 010 kg/hr) = (0.05x106x3010)/60 kg = 265.88 kg
 
Alternate fuel (provided by the question) = 1 455 kg
 
Final reserve fuel (provided by the question) = 1 360 kg
 
As a result, we have: sum (d)(1) = 18 812.5 kg + 265.88 kg + 1 455 kg + 1 360 kg = 21 893.38 kg
 
sum (d)(2):
Trip fuel from departure to destination 2 aerodrome, via the decision point = Flight time from the departure aerodrome to the destination 2 aerodrome, via the decision point x Average fuel flow = (315/60 hr) x (3 010 kg/hr) = (315x3010)/60 kg = 15 802.5 kg
 
Contingency fuel should be the higher of:
 
5% of the trip fuel from the departure aerodrome to the destination 2 aerodrome = 0.05 x 15 802.5 kg = 790.125 kg   or
     
The fuel required to hold for 5 min at 1 500ft (assume the average fuel flow of 3 010kg/hr) = (5x3010)/60 kg = 250.83 kg

As a result, Contingency fuel = 790.125 kg
 
Alternate fuel (provided by the question) = 1 906 kg
 
Final reserve fuel (provided by the question) = 1 360 kg
 
As a result, we have: sum (d)(2) = 15 802.5 kg + 790.125 kg + 1 906 kg + 1 360 kg = 19 858.625 kg
 
We notice that the sum (d)(1) is greater than the sum(d)(2). As a result, the minimum take-off fuel required comes from sum (d)(1), i.e. the minimum take-off fuel required is: 21 893.38 kg (closest answer: 21 894 kg)

Æ Note 1: Flight times:
 
Flight time from the decision point to the destination 1 aerodrome = 1 hr 46 min or 106 min or 106/60 hr.
 
Flight time from the departure aerodrome to the destination 1 aerodrome, via the decision point = flight time from the departure aerodrome to the decision point + flight time from the decision point to the destination 1 aerodrome = 4 hr 29 min + 1 hr 46 min = 6 hr 15 min or 375 min or 375/60 hr or 6.25 hr.
 
Flight time from the departure aerodrome to the destination 2 aerodrome, via the decision point = flight time from the departure aerodrome to the decision point + flight time from the decision point to the destination 2 aerodrome = 4 hr 29 min + 46 min = 5 hr 15 min or 315 min or 315/60 hr or 5.25 hr.
 
Æ Note 2: In the calculation of the contingency fuel in sum (b)(2), we used the 5% of the trip fuel from the departure aerodrome to the destination 2 aerodrome. This was done because the suitable en-route alternate available for contingency fuel purposes is established only for destination 1 aerodrome. If the suitable en-route alternate available for contingency fuel purposes was valid for destination 2 aerodrome, then we would use the 3%. Furthermore, we calculated the fuel required to fly for 5 minutes at holding speed using the given average fuel flow, despite the fact that the question does not give the fuel flow at holding speed. For the calculation of average fuel flow, the fuel flow at holding speed has been taken into consideration. 

Æ Note 3: According to the EASA AIR OPS regulations, no alternate fuel is required when the DP is less than 6 hours away from the destination 1. However, requirements CAT.OP.MPA.180 (b)(2) also need to be fulfilled. As this is not mentioned in the question, we therefore include the alternate fuel.

The purpose of the decision point procedure is?
options: A) To reduce the landing weight and thus reduce the structural stress on the aircraft.
B) To increase the safety of the flight.
C) To increase the amount of extra fuel.
D) To reduce the minimum required fuel and therefore be able to increase the Traffic Load.
correct_answer: D) To reduce the minimum required fuel and therefore be able to increase the Traffic Load.

question: You are planning an IFR flight where a destination alternate is not possible, what is the regulatory fuel overhead the destination? You must carry...
options: A) No additional fuel, but you need to double the contingency fuel.
B) No additional fuel and no contingency fuel.
C) Discretionary fuel, only if required by the commander.
D) Additional fuel, and you need to apply the isolated aerodrome procedure.
correct_answer: D) Additional fuel, and you need to apply the isolated aerodrome procedure.

explanation:
Fuel tankering involves intentionally carrying more fuel than necessary for a flight segment to avoid refueling at a destination where fuel prices are higher, thereby potentially achieving cost savings, while also considering the trade-off of increased fuel consumption due to the added weight of the extra fuel.
is correct. For the reasons stated above, this is basically what fuel tankering is.
is correct. The intention is to approach the planned destination, and in case of the weather still being below minimum, use the fuel to fly somewhere else – not for a planned return. This is not exactly fuel tankering by definition. Anyway, as it is possible to consider that we put more fuel before taking-off due to this, it may be considered a correct answer.

B) Yes, if you proceed to a holding, to lose 30 kg, to be able to complete the landing.
C) The take-off is not possible under this condition.
D) No need to hold, there's no operational impact.

A pilot plans ATH→CDG→ATH and decides to carry enough fuel for both legs from Athens to avoid refuelling in Paris. Which of the following statements is correct, assuming the conditions remain unchanged on both legs?
options: A) The fuel consumption will be less from ATH to CDG.
B) The fuel consumption will be the same for both legs.
C) The CO₂ emission on the outbound leg (ATH→CDG) will be higher.
D) The take-off mass on the inbound leg (CDG→ATH) will be higher.
correct_answer: C) The CO₂ emission on the outbound leg (ATH→CDG) will be higher.

question: When planning a flight according to the predetermined point procedure, what is the minimum amount of fuel that a turbine-engined aircraft needs to carry? The greater of
options:
A) 1. Taxi fuel, trip fuel to destination + contingency, additional fuel if required for at least 45 min + 15% of flight time at cruising level or 2 hours, whichever is less and not less than final reserve fuel
2. Taxi fuel, trip fuel to the destination alternate + contingency, additional fuel if required but at least fuel to fly for 45 minutes and not less than final reserve fuel

B) 1. Taxi fuel, trip fuel to destination + contingency, additional fuel if required but at least 2 hours at cruise consumption above the destination and not less than final reserve fuel
2. Taxi fuel, trip fuel to the destination alternate + contingency, additional fuel if required but at least 30 minutes at holding speed at 1 500 ft above alternate elevation and not less than final reserve fuel

C) 1. Taxi fuel, trip fuel to destination + contingency, fuel to destination alternate, final reserve fuel
2. Taxi fuel, trip fuel to the alternate + contingency, fuel from alternate back to destination, final reserve fuel

D) 1. Taxi fuel, trip fuel to predetermined point via destination, trip fuel from predetermined point to alternate, final reserve fuel
2. Taxi fuel, trip fuel to predetermined point via alternate, trip fuel from predetermined point to destination, final reserve fuel
correct_answer:
B) 1. Taxi fuel, trip fuel to destination + contingency, additional fuel if required but at least 2 hours at cruise consumption above the destination and not less than final reserve fuel
2. Taxi fuel, trip fuel to the destination alternate + contingency, additional fuel if required but at least 30 minutes at holding speed at 1 500 ft above alternate elevation and not less than final reserve fuel

question: A flight crew is using the 'Reduced Contingency Fuel (Decision Point) Procedure' to reduce...
options: A) contingency fuel, and therefore to increase the final reserve fuel.
B) minimum required fuel, and therefore to increase the amount of additional fuel.
C) minimum required fuel, and therefore to increase the load.
D) contingency fuel, and therefore to increase the amount of possible extra fuel.
correct_answer: C) minimum required fuel, and therefore to increase the load.

question: Under what circumstances regarding fuel costs is tankering between airports a recommended strategy for an aircraft flying from Airport A to Airport B and back?
options: A) Fuel price at Airport A must be higher than at Airport B to minimize the overall fuel cost.
B) Fuel price at Airport A must be lower than at Airport B to reduce the aircraft's gross mass at take-off.
C) Fuel price at Airport B must be significantly higher than at Airport A to compensate for the increased fuel burn due to extra mass.
D) Fuel price at Airport B must be equal to or higher than at Airport A to justify tankering.
correct_answer: C) Fuel price at Airport B must be significantly higher than at Airport A to compensate for the increased fuel burn due to extra mass.

question: According to the NOTAM, fluctuations of the GNSS signal reception will take place during the RNP approach at the destination airfield at the time of arrival. What should the pilot of the commercial flight do?
options: A) Cancel the flight and reschedule it when the GNSS signal reception is fully functioning as an RNP approach must be flown.
B) In the event of a GNSS signal loss during flight, discontinue the approach and initiate a go-around. Then, prepare for another approach using radio navigation aids.
C) Postpone the flight because the lack of GNSS achievability is very dangerous.
D) Carry on with the flight as there are two GPS receivers on board, which can compensate for signal fluctuation.
correct_answer: B) In the event of a GNSS signal loss during flight, discontinue the approach and initiate a go-around. Then, prepare for another approach using radio navigation aids.

question:
A1014/23 NOTAMR A0473/23 Q) LCCC/QGWXX/IV/NBO/E/000/999/3426N03246E171 A) LCCC B) 2307030723 C) 2310022359 E) GNSS SIGNAL INTERRUPTIONS HAVE BEEN REPORTED WITHIN NICOSIA FIR. PILOTS ARE REQUESTED TO REPORT TO ATC. WITHIN TMA CONVENTIONAL INSTRUMENT PROCEDURES ARE AVAILABLE ON PILOT REQUEST

Consider the above NOTAM. A pilot who is planning to fly to a destination within Nicosia FIR within the affected dates:
options: A) cannot plan to use any GNSS-based procedure as there are planned outages of GNSS necessitating the use of conventional instrument procedures.
B) may plan to use GNSS-based procedures but must consider contingency plans in the event of any issues with the GNSS signals.
C) cannot use GNSS for position referencing since the signals are unreliable and there are no backup alternatives in the event of loss of GNSS signals.
D) may use GNSS-based procedures without restrictions as ATC will have a record of when any signal outages are planned to occur.
correct_answer: B) may plan to use GNSS-based procedures but must consider contingency plans in the event of any issues with the GNSS signals.

question: What is the difference between GNSS planned and unplanned outages?
options: A) Notifications are issued only after the recovery of an unplanned outage.
B) Notifications are issued only after the end of a planned outage.
C) Unplanned outages will always be notified in advance.
D) Planned outages will always be notified in advance.
correct_answer: D) Planned outages will always be notified in advance.

question: Where can you, as a pilot, find information about the GNSS, which is not available at the destination airfield?
If RAIM was verified during flight preparation, should NOTAMs also be verified, and why?
options: A) Do not check NOTAMs because RAIM concerns both EGNOS and all GNSS, making additional checks unnecessary.
B) Check NOTAMs specifically for updates on LNAV/VNAV that RAIM does not cover.
C) Check NOTAMs because RAIM is only about EGNOS and does not cover all GNSS issues.
D) Always check NOTAMs, as RAIM predictions might not include temporary changes affecting GNSS services like those documented in NOTAMs.
correct_answer: D) Always check NOTAMs, as RAIM predictions might not include temporary changes affecting GNSS services like those documented in NOTAMs.

question:
Refer to the following NOTAM. Are you allowed to fly to EUAD?

Q) EGTT/QAZCA/IV/NBO/AE/000/022/5334N00021W003 A) EUAD B) FROM: 15/12/08 09:42 C) PERM E) AERODROME ATZ ACTIVE 0620-2115 DAILY. AD OPEN, ATS AVBL, USE OF AD IS PRIOR PERMISSION REQUIRED 0620-0630 UTC FOR NON SKED MOVEMENTS. EUAD AD 2.3 AND AD 2.17 REFER
options: A) Yes, you can fly to EUAD at any time between 06:20-21:15 UTC daily.
B) Yes, but you must file a flight plan before departing, at least two hours before the scheduled arrival time.
C) Yes, but prior permission is required between 06:20-21:15.
D) Yes, but you need prior permission for non scheduled flights between 06:20 and 06:30 and after that time you can fly without permission until 21:15.
correct_answer: D) Yes, but you need prior permission for non scheduled flights between 06:20 and 06:30 and after that time you can fly without permission until 21:15.

A) EBBR
B) 1912041845
C) 1912042045
E) EGNOS IS NOT AVAILABLE FOR LPV
options: A) You won't be affected at the time of arrival.
B) ABAS will be unavailable for all approaches.
C) You will not be able to use SBAS for LPV minima.
D) You can not execute 3D RNAV approaches to Hamburg due to the SBAS outage.
correct_answer: C) You will not be able to use SBAS for LPV minima.

correct_answer: B) During the flight, and the pilot must be prepared for an alternative approach.

options: A) Yes, if the airfield has one. A TACAN has nothing to do with DME equipment.
B) Yes, it is well within range.
C) No, the facility is down for maintenance.
D) No, as there is no such facility at Mildenhall.
correct_answer: C) No, the facility is down for maintenance.

question:

B) Only under VFR, as airways are excluded from the effects of such notifications.

correct_answer: D) Yes, the information in the NOTAM is a factor.

Which statement best explains what a basic RAIM check verifies with regard to SBAS availability?
options: A) Basic RAIM checks SBAS availability automatically if the aircraft is equipped with WAAS/EGNOS receivers.
B) Basic RAIM is no longer required when flying in SBAS-covered areas.
C) Basic RAIM checks GNSS satellite geometry and also verifies SBAS augmentation availability.
D) Basic RAIM only checks GNSS availability; it does not check SBAS augmentation, which must be verified separately (e.g., via NOTAM).

question:
A pilot is planning a flight through the Vienna FIR (LOVV) on 20th March 2026. The following NOTAM is retrieved during pre-flight preparation:

A2847/26 NOTAMN
Q) LOVV/QGAAS/IV/NBO/AE/080/380/4812N01624E999
A) LOVV
B) 2603150000
C) 2603242300
D) 1900 2300
E) GPS/GNSS MAY BE UNRELIABLE DUE TO MILITARY EXERCISES WITHIN:
   40NM RADIUS AT FL380 AND ABOVE
   25NM RADIUS AT FL250
   15NM RADIUS AT FL150
   8NM RADIUS AT FL080
   CENTRED ON N48°12' E016°24'

Which of the following statements is correct?
options: A) GNSS will not be available within the affected area between 15/03 and 24/03, every day from 1900 to 2300 UTC.
B) GNSS may be unreliable within the affected area every day between 15/03 and 24/03 from 1900 to 2300 UTC.
C) GNSS will be unavailable throughout the Vienna FIR from 15/03 until 24/03.
D) GNSS will be out of service on 20/03 at all flight levels within 40 NM of N48°12' E016°24'.
correct_answer: B) GNSS may be unreliable within the affected area every day between 15/03 and 24/03 from 1900 to 2300 UTC.

You’re in cruise in a turbojet aircraft, approaching top-of-descent, when GNSS capability is lost. What is the correct action?
options: A) Continue the planned arrival; no action is needed until the approach.
B) Begin descent and continue the planned RNAV arrival using dead-reckoning.
C) Maintain current level while you positively fix your position with other nav sources (e.g., VOR/DME/INS) before commencing the descent, and advise ATC.
D) Descend to MSA and proceed visually without advising ATC.
correct_answer: C) Maintain current level while you positively fix your position with other nav sources (e.g., VOR/DME/INS) before commencing the descent, and advise ATC.

options: A) The availability of satellites is dependent on ground-based navigation aids.
B) Satellites may NOT be available due to the status of the constellation.
C) The availability of satellites is dependent on the aircraft's equipment.
D) Satellites may NOT be available due to terrain and/or antenna masking.
correct_answer: B) Satellites may NOT be available due to the status of the constellation.

question: A NOTAM states BASIC GNSS UNAVBL in a given area on Jan 5 from 17:30 to 20:00 UTC. What does this imply for aircraft operating in that area and time period?
options: A) GNSS may still be used en-route, but is prohibited only for instrument approaches during 17:30 to 20:00 UTC on Jan 5.
B) Only SBAS corrections are unavailable; basic GNSS remains authorized for en-route and approach, provided RAIM is available.
C) The NOTAM is advisory; pilots may continue to use GNSS if a preflight RAIM prediction shows availability.
D) Aircraft cannot rely on GNSS for navigation during 17:30 to 20:00 UTC on Jan 5 and must use alternative means of navigation.
correct_answer: D) Aircraft cannot rely on GNSS for navigation during 17:30 to 20:00 UTC on Jan 5 and must use alternative means of navigation.

options: A) The FMS will give the crew groundspeed, and the change in groundspeed can be used to adjust the ETO at OSLEL.
B) The FMS will give the crew a distance and groundspeed, which can be used to calculate ETO at OSLEL to check against the clearance.
C) The FMS will give the crew a new ETO at OSLEL, which can be checked against the time in the clearance.
D) The FMS will give the crew a time to run to OSLEL, from which they can confirm the ETO is correct.
correct_answer: C) The FMS will give the crew a new ETO at OSLEL, which can be checked against the time in the clearance.

A turbojet aircraft, while in cruise and approaching the top of descent, it loses GNSS capability. Which action by the pilot is the most correct ?
options: A) Begin descent and continue the planned RNAV arrival using dead-reckoning.
B) Maintain current level while you positively fix your position with other nav sources (e.g., VOR/DME/INS) before commencing the descent, and advise ATC.
C) Continue the planned arrival; no action is needed until the approach.
D) Descend to MSA and proceed visually without advising ATC.
correct_answer: B) Maintain current level while you positively fix your position with other nav sources (e.g., VOR/DME/INS) before commencing the descent, and advise ATC.

A multi engine piston aeroplane is on an IFR flight. The fuel plan gives a Trip Fuel of 65 US gallons. The alternate fuel, final reserve included, is 17 US gallons. Contingency fuel is 5% of the Trip Fuel. The usable fuel at departure is 93 US gallons. At a certain moment the fuel consumed according to the fuel gauges is 40 US gallons and the distance flown is half of the total distance. Assume that fuel consumption does not change. Which statement is right?
options: A) At departure the reserve fuel was 28 US gallons.
B) At the destination there will still be 30 US gallons in the tanks.
C) At destination the required reserves remain intact.
D) The remaining fuel is not sufficient to reach the destination with reserves intact.
correct_answer: D) The remaining fuel is not sufficient to reach the destination with reserves intact.

options: B) BERMUDAS, GANDER, or SANTA MARIA.
correct_answer: B) BERMUDAS, GANDER, or SANTA MARIA.

question: Consider a commercial air transport flight. Is it allowed to use the final reserve fuel?
options: A) When there are safe forced landing areas along the entire route to be flown.
B) This is prohibited as such fuel is for emergency use only.
C) In case of an unexpected diversion.
D) For all VFR flights by day.
correct_answer: B) This is prohibited as such fuel is for emergency use only.

question: On approach in VMC, ATS informs you that you have to hold for 17 minutes, having a final reserve fuel for 30 minutes, an alternate fuel of 40 minutes and extra fuel for 10 minutes. What is the most legal and commercially possible option?
options: A) Declare immediately low on fuel so that ATC will give you priority to land.
B) Immediately proceed to the alternate, leaving the 30 minutes of final reserve fuel intact and extra fuel on landing.
C) Hold and land at destination.
D) Hold 10 minutes to see effectively if it's a 17 minutes holding, and in case it is, proceed to the alternate, leaving the 30 minutes of final reserve fuel intact.
correct_answer: D) Hold 10 minutes to see effectively if it's a 17 minutes holding, and in case it is, proceed to the alternate, leaving the 30 minutes of final reserve fuel intact.

question:

Calculate the revised estimated landing mass at the destination aerodrome.

Given the following information for a Commercial Air Transport flight, what action should be taken by the commander?
 
Take-off fuel:	3 300 lb
Trip fuel:	1 800 lb
Alternate 1 fuel:	800 lb
Alternate 2 fuel:	500 lb
Final reserve fuel:	600 lb
Total distance to destination:	800 NM
Remaining fuel after 400 NM:	2 300 lb
options: A) Declare an emergency as the remaining fuel at the Alternate 2 is less than the Final reserve fuel.
B) Declare an emergency immediately as the remaining fuel at the Alternate 1 is less than the Final reserve fuel.
C) Continue the flight; the remaining fuel at the Alternate 1 will be more than the Final reserve fuel.
D) Continue the flight; in case of diversion proceed to the Alternate 2.
correct_answer: D) Continue the flight; in case of diversion proceed to the Alternate 2.
